revision.comment: Text replacement - "↵↵== See Also ==" to "== See Also =="
revision.text:
'''Shuto''' (衆徒, しゅと), also known as '''Dōshu''' (堂衆, どうしゅ/どうしゅう), originally refers to the class of Buddhist monks who, from the Heian period onward, resided in large temples and, in addition to engaging in scholarly study and ascetic training, performed the practical administrative duties within the temple. They formed the main body of the temple’s "great multitude" ({{ill|Daisū|ja|大衆}}), and in some contexts the term "daisū" is used exclusively to refer to these shuto. In particular, at {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}} in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}}, where the temple also served a protective function ({{ill|Shugo|ja|守護職}}), the warriors of the country (referred to as ''Yamato Bushi'' (大和武士)) were sometimes called shuto.
== Overview == In general, shuto rank below the scholarly monks ({{ill|Gakuryo|ja|学侶}}) and above the itinerant monks ({{ill|Gyōnin|ja|行人}}). They handled practical tasks within the temple such as security, management, and overall operations. Many shuto came from mid-to-lower class aristocratic families, samurai, or local officials ({{ill|Shōkan|ja|荘官}}), and some possessed martial skills; indeed, armed monk soldiers ({{ill|Sōhei|ja|僧兵}}) were largely composed of shuto. (However, at {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}}, the shuto were predominantly drawn from higher-ranking nobles, and in that temple those expelled from the shuto class organized separately as "dōshu.") They played a central role in temple decision-making and in the enforcement of the temple’s authority (through bodies such as the {{ill|Daisū Shingi|ja|大衆僉議}} and {{ill|Kyōso|ja|強訴}}). Later, as monastic hierarchies became more differentiated, scholarly monks ({{ill|Gakuryo|ja|学侶}}) who were of high-ranking court families ({{ill|Kōkei|ja|Kugyō}}) attempted to monopolize key positions and to influence temple management, leading to fierce opposition from the itinerant monks.
== Yamato Warriors == In Yamato Province, where {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}} had strong connections with the {{ill|Sesseki family|ja|摂関家}}, the temple, upon entering the Kamakura period, eventually secured control over Nara and later over the entire Yamato region. Originally, the shuto of this temple were fixed at twenty by the imperial edict (via the {{ill|Taihōkanfu|ja|太政官符}}), serving as assistants to the bettō and the Sangkō. Over time, however, influential temple families ({{ill|Monzeki|ja|門跡}}) such as those affiliated with {{ill|Ichijō-in|ja|一乗院}} and {{ill|Daijō-in|ja|大乗院}} expanded their power by incorporating domestic warriors and local chieftains (often styled as {{ill|Gokenin|ja|御家人}}) into their own retinues, subsequently leading their scholarly monks to assume administrative and security roles within the temple and in the judicial matters ({{ill|Kendan|ja|検断}}) of Nara. Additionally, through the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, the "divine men" ({{ill|Shinjin|ja|神人}}) of Kasuga Shrine, which had become integrated with Kōfuku-ji, were similarly incorporated, and these Kasuga deities came to be known as "kokumin" (国民).
Notable Yamato shuto include those under the patronage of Ichijō-in—such as members of the {{ill|Tutsui Clan|ja|筒井氏}} and the {{ill|Ochi Clan|ja|越智氏}}—and those under Daijō-in—such as members of the {{ill|Toshichi Clan|ja|十市氏}} and the {{ill|Furuichi Clan|ja|古市氏}}. In the Nanboku-chō period, Tōshichi Tōyasu invaded Kōfuku-ji’s estates and established his own domain as a local lord. In response to a request from the bettō of Kōfuku-ji for the suppression of Tōshichi Tōyasu, the 3rd shogun of the Muromachi Bakufu, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|ja|足利義満}}, dispatched commanders such as {{ill|Shiba Yoshishiro|ja|斯波義将}} and {{ill|Toki Yoriyasu|ja|土岐頼康}} to Yamato in 1379 (Eiwa 5). However, when the Bakufu army showed no signs of commencing an expedition against Tōshichi Tōyasu, Yoshimitsu ordered all the generals of the Tōshichi suppression force to return to Kyoto. Consequently, for the first time in history, the protest by the "Jinboku Nyūroku" (the entry of the sacred tree into the capital) failed, dealing a severe blow to temple power. The 6th shogun, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|ja|足利義教}}, intervened in the personnel decisions of the influential temple families within Yamato Province in order to establish Bakufu authority. However, due to conflicts between the factions of Tōdaichū-bō and Ido, the situation devolved into a quagmire. In Tōdaichū-bō, factions including Ochi, Hashio, and Manzai allied with Southern Court elements such as Sawa and Akiyama emerged, while in Ido, the Tutsui and Toshichi factions prevailed; the rivalry between the Tutsui and Ochi factions eventually split Yamato into two, sparking the conflict known as the "Yamato Eikyō no Ran." In August of the 3rd year of the {{ill|Bunmei era|ja|文明}} (1471), forces led by Fuse (Eastern faction) and Ochi Ie Saka (Western faction) clashed—with Fuse supported by Hashio, Narahara, Kubishira, Toshichi, Tutsui, and Maetaka (Fuse Takada), and Ochi reinforced by Manzai, Hatta, Iitaka, Furuichi, Todata, Koizumi, and Tōtakada (Tōma Takada). With influential guardian daimyō also intervening, the long internal conflict, interspersed with the Ōnin War, eventually ushered in a period of warlord rule during the Sengoku period in Yamato Province.
During the Sengoku period, following the death in battle of [[Hatakeyama clan]] retainer [[Kizawa Nagamasa]] in Kawachi Province, the clans led by {{ill|Tutsui Junshō|ja|筒井氏}} and {{ill|Ochi Clan|ja|越智氏}} expanded their influence. For a time they controlled much of the country and advanced toward becoming warlords, but after Junshō’s sudden death and the succession by his young heir, {{ill|Tutsui Junnin|ja|十市遠康}}, the Ochi and Furuichi clans counterattacked, leading to the disintegration of Tutsui control. Seizing the opportunity, Matsunaga Hisahide, under the Southern Court during the Nanboku-chō period, invaded Yamato and expelled Junnin, subduing the Ochi clan and others, and in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永和}} (1559) seized the position of provincial protector (Shugo) from Kōfuku-ji. Later, under the Toyotomi regime, Tutsui Junnin reemerged as a warlord in Yamato Province and established a dual system whereby he simultaneously held the status of protector of Yamato Province and acted as the representative of the government-appointed (kanfu) shuto—the temple retainer class—in administering and safeguarding the temple. However, after Junnin's death, Toyotomi Hideyoshi granted Yamato Province to his brother Hidechō, thereby disbanding the existing system, forcing the shuto and the local "kokumin" (the people of the country) to be dissolved; and for Kōfuku-ji, only the original twenty names (as recorded on the official kanfu shuto list) were allowed to serve as temple administrators and security personnel, with a total stipend of 380 koku.== See Also == * {{ill|Yamato Eikyō no Ran|ja|大和永享の乱}} * {{ill|Warrior Monk|ja|ウォリアーモンク}}

'''Shuto''' (衆徒, しゅと), also known as '''Dōshu''' (堂衆, どうしゅ/どうしゅう), originally refers to the class of Buddhist monks who, from the Heian period onward, resided in large temples and, in addition to engaging in scholarly study and ascetic training, performed the practical administrative duties within the temple. They formed the main body of the temple’s "great multitude" ({{ill|Daisū|ja|大衆}}), and in some contexts the term "daisū" is used exclusively to refer to these shuto. In particular, at {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}} in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}}, where the temple also served a protective function ({{ill|Shugo|ja|守護職}}), the warriors of the country (referred to as ''Yamato Bushi'' (大和武士)) were sometimes called shuto.
== Overview == In general, shuto rank below the scholarly monks ({{ill|Gakuryo|ja|学侶}}) and above the itinerant monks ({{ill|Gyōnin|ja|行人}}). They handled practical tasks within the temple such as security, management, and overall operations. Many shuto came from mid-to-lower class aristocratic families, samurai, or local officials ({{ill|Shōkan|ja|荘官}}), and some possessed martial skills; indeed, armed monk soldiers ({{ill|Sōhei|ja|僧兵}}) were largely composed of shuto. (However, at {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}}, the shuto were predominantly drawn from higher-ranking nobles, and in that temple those expelled from the shuto class organized separately as "dōshu.") They played a central role in temple decision-making and in the enforcement of the temple’s authority (through bodies such as the {{ill|Daisū Shingi|ja|大衆僉議}} and {{ill|Kyōso|ja|強訴}}). Later, as monastic hierarchies became more differentiated, scholarly monks ({{ill|Gakuryo|ja|学侶}}) who were of high-ranking court families ({{ill|Kōkei|ja|Kugyō}}) attempted to monopolize key positions and to influence temple management, leading to fierce opposition from the itinerant monks.
== Yamato Warriors == In Yamato Province, where {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}} had strong connections with the {{ill|Sesseki family|ja|摂関家}}, the temple, upon entering the Kamakura period, eventually secured control over Nara and later over the entire Yamato region. Originally, the shuto of this temple were fixed at twenty by the imperial edict (via the {{ill|Taihōkanfu|ja|太政官符}}), serving as assistants to the bettō and the Sangkō. Over time, however, influential temple families ({{ill|Monzeki|ja|門跡}}) such as those affiliated with {{ill|Ichijō-in|ja|一乗院}} and {{ill|Daijō-in|ja|大乗院}} expanded their power by incorporating domestic warriors and local chieftains (often styled as {{ill|Gokenin|ja|御家人}}) into their own retinues, subsequently leading their scholarly monks to assume administrative and security roles within the temple and in the judicial matters ({{ill|Kendan|ja|検断}}) of Nara. Additionally, through the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, the "divine men" ({{ill|Shinjin|ja|神人}}) of Kasuga Shrine, which had become integrated with Kōfuku-ji, were similarly incorporated, and these Kasuga deities came to be known as "kokumin" (国民).
Notable Yamato shuto include those under the patronage of Ichijō-in—such as members of the {{ill|Tutsui Clan|ja|筒井氏}} and the {{ill|Ochi Clan|ja|越智氏}}—and those under Daijō-in—such as members of the {{ill|Toshichi Clan|ja|十市氏}} and the {{ill|Furuichi Clan|ja|古市氏}}. In the Nanboku-chō period, Tōshichi Tōyasu invaded Kōfuku-ji’s estates and established his own domain as a local lord. In response to a request from the bettō of Kōfuku-ji for the suppression of Tōshichi Tōyasu, the 3rd shogun of the Muromachi Bakufu, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|ja|足利義満}}, dispatched commanders such as {{ill|Shiba Yoshishiro|ja|斯波義将}} and {{ill|Toki Yoriyasu|ja|土岐頼康}} to Yamato in 1379 (Eiwa 5). However, when the Bakufu army showed no signs of commencing an expedition against Tōshichi Tōyasu, Yoshimitsu ordered all the generals of the Tōshichi suppression force to return to Kyoto. Consequently, for the first time in history, the protest by the "Jinboku Nyūroku" (the entry of the sacred tree into the capital) failed, dealing a severe blow to temple power. The 6th shogun, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|ja|足利義教}}, intervened in the personnel decisions of the influential temple families within Yamato Province in order to establish Bakufu authority. However, due to conflicts between the factions of Tōdaichū-bō and Ido, the situation devolved into a quagmire. In Tōdaichū-bō, factions including Ochi, Hashio, and Manzai allied with Southern Court elements such as Sawa and Akiyama emerged, while in Ido, the Tutsui and Toshichi factions prevailed; the rivalry between the Tutsui and Ochi factions eventually split Yamato into two, sparking the conflict known as the "Yamato Eikyō no Ran." In August of the 3rd year of the {{ill|Bunmei era|ja|文明}} (1471), forces led by Fuse (Eastern faction) and Ochi Ie Saka (Western faction) clashed—with Fuse supported by Hashio, Narahara, Kubishira, Toshichi, Tutsui, and Maetaka (Fuse Takada), and Ochi reinforced by Manzai, Hatta, Iitaka, Furuichi, Todata, Koizumi, and Tōtakada (Tōma Takada). With influential guardian daimyō also intervening, the long internal conflict, interspersed with the Ōnin War, eventually ushered in a period of warlord rule during the Sengoku period in Yamato Province.
During the Sengoku period, following the death in battle of [[Hatakeyama clan]] retainer [[Kizawa Nagamasa]] in Kawachi Province, the clans led by {{ill|Tutsui Junshō|ja|筒井氏}} and {{ill|Ochi Clan|ja|越智氏}} expanded their influence. For a time they controlled much of the country and advanced toward becoming warlords, but after Junshō’s sudden death and the succession by his young heir, {{ill|Tutsui Junnin|ja|十市遠康}}, the Ochi and Furuichi clans counterattacked, leading to the disintegration of Tutsui control. Seizing the opportunity, Matsunaga Hisahide, under the Southern Court during the Nanboku-chō period, invaded Yamato and expelled Junnin, subduing the Ochi clan and others, and in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永和}} (1559) seized the position of provincial protector (Shugo) from Kōfuku-ji. Later, under the Toyotomi regime, Tutsui Junnin reemerged as a warlord in Yamato Province and established a dual system whereby he simultaneously held the status of protector of Yamato Province and acted as the representative of the government-appointed (kanfu) shuto—the temple retainer class—in administering and safeguarding the temple. However, after Junnin's death, Toyotomi Hideyoshi granted Yamato Province to his brother Hidechō, thereby disbanding the existing system, forcing the shuto and the local "kokumin" (the people of the country) to be dissolved; and for Kōfuku-ji, only the original twenty names (as recorded on the official kanfu shuto list) were allowed to serve as temple administrators and security personnel, with a total stipend of 380 koku.== See Also == * {{ill|Yamato Eikyō no Ran|ja|大和永享の乱}} * {{ill|Warrior Monk|ja|ウォリアーモンク}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "== See Also ==" to " == See Also! =="
revision.text:
'''Shuto''' (衆徒, しゅと), also known as '''Dōshu''' (堂衆, どうしゅ/どうしゅう), originally refers to the class of Buddhist monks who, from the Heian period onward, resided in large temples and, in addition to engaging in scholarly study and ascetic training, performed the practical administrative duties within the temple. They formed the main body of the temple’s "great multitude" ({{ill|Daisū|ja|大衆}}), and in some contexts the term "daisū" is used exclusively to refer to these shuto. In particular, at {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}} in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}}, where the temple also served a protective function ({{ill|Shugo|ja|守護職}}), the warriors of the country (referred to as ''Yamato Bushi'' (大和武士)) were sometimes called shuto.
== Overview == In general, shuto rank below the scholarly monks ({{ill|Gakuryo|ja|学侶}}) and above the itinerant monks ({{ill|Gyōnin|ja|行人}}). They handled practical tasks within the temple such as security, management, and overall operations. Many shuto came from mid-to-lower class aristocratic families, samurai, or local officials ({{ill|Shōkan|ja|荘官}}), and some possessed martial skills; indeed, armed monk soldiers ({{ill|Sōhei|ja|僧兵}}) were largely composed of shuto. (However, at {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}}, the shuto were predominantly drawn from higher-ranking nobles, and in that temple those expelled from the shuto class organized separately as "dōshu.") They played a central role in temple decision-making and in the enforcement of the temple’s authority (through bodies such as the {{ill|Daisū Shingi|ja|大衆僉議}} and {{ill|Kyōso|ja|強訴}}). Later, as monastic hierarchies became more differentiated, scholarly monks ({{ill|Gakuryo|ja|学侶}}) who were of high-ranking court families ({{ill|Kōkei|ja|Kugyō}}) attempted to monopolize key positions and to influence temple management, leading to fierce opposition from the itinerant monks.
== Yamato Warriors == In Yamato Province, where {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}} had strong connections with the {{ill|Sesseki family|ja|摂関家}}, the temple, upon entering the Kamakura period, eventually secured control over Nara and later over the entire Yamato region. Originally, the shuto of this temple were fixed at twenty by the imperial edict (via the {{ill|Taihōkanfu|ja|太政官符}}), serving as assistants to the bettō and the Sangkō. Over time, however, influential temple families ({{ill|Monzeki|ja|門跡}}) such as those affiliated with {{ill|Ichijō-in|ja|一乗院}} and {{ill|Daijō-in|ja|大乗院}} expanded their power by incorporating domestic warriors and local chieftains (often styled as {{ill|Gokenin|ja|御家人}}) into their own retinues, subsequently leading their scholarly monks to assume administrative and security roles within the temple and in the judicial matters ({{ill|Kendan|ja|検断}}) of Nara. Additionally, through the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, the "divine men" ({{ill|Shinjin|ja|神人}}) of Kasuga Shrine, which had become integrated with Kōfuku-ji, were similarly incorporated, and these Kasuga deities came to be known as "kokumin" (国民).
Notable Yamato shuto include those under the patronage of Ichijō-in—such as members of the {{ill|Tutsui Clan|ja|筒井氏}} and the {{ill|Ochi Clan|ja|越智氏}}—and those under Daijō-in—such as members of the {{ill|Toshichi Clan|ja|十市氏}} and the {{ill|Furuichi Clan|ja|古市氏}}. In the Nanboku-chō period, Tōshichi Tōyasu invaded Kōfuku-ji’s estates and established his own domain as a local lord. In response to a request from the bettō of Kōfuku-ji for the suppression of Tōshichi Tōyasu, the 3rd shogun of the Muromachi Bakufu, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|ja|足利義満}}, dispatched commanders such as {{ill|Shiba Yoshishiro|ja|斯波義将}} and {{ill|Toki Yoriyasu|ja|土岐頼康}} to Yamato in 1379 (Eiwa 5). However, when the Bakufu army showed no signs of commencing an expedition against Tōshichi Tōyasu, Yoshimitsu ordered all the generals of the Tōshichi suppression force to return to Kyoto. Consequently, for the first time in history, the protest by the "Jinboku Nyūroku" (the entry of the sacred tree into the capital) failed, dealing a severe blow to temple power. The 6th shogun, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|ja|足利義教}}, intervened in the personnel decisions of the influential temple families within Yamato Province in order to establish Bakufu authority. However, due to conflicts between the factions of Tōdaichū-bō and Ido, the situation devolved into a quagmire. In Tōdaichū-bō, factions including Ochi, Hashio, and Manzai allied with Southern Court elements such as Sawa and Akiyama emerged, while in Ido, the Tutsui and Toshichi factions prevailed; the rivalry between the Tutsui and Ochi factions eventually split Yamato into two, sparking the conflict known as the "Yamato Eikyō no Ran." In August of the 3rd year of the {{ill|Bunmei era|ja|文明}} (1471), forces led by Fuse (Eastern faction) and Ochi Ie Saka (Western faction) clashed—with Fuse supported by Hashio, Narahara, Kubishira, Toshichi, Tutsui, and Maetaka (Fuse Takada), and Ochi reinforced by Manzai, Hatta, Iitaka, Furuichi, Todata, Koizumi, and Tōtakada (Tōma Takada). With influential guardian daimyō also intervening, the long internal conflict, interspersed with the Ōnin War, eventually ushered in a period of warlord rule during the Sengoku period in Yamato Province.
During the Sengoku period, following the death in battle of [[Hatakeyama clan]] retainer [[Kizawa Nagamasa]] in Kawachi Province, the clans led by {{ill|Tutsui Junshō|ja|筒井氏}} and {{ill|Ochi Clan|ja|越智氏}} expanded their influence. For a time they controlled much of the country and advanced toward becoming warlords, but after Junshō’s sudden death and the succession by his young heir, {{ill|Tutsui Junnin|ja|十市遠康}}, the Ochi and Furuichi clans counterattacked, leading to the disintegration of Tutsui control. Seizing the opportunity, Matsunaga Hisahide, under the Southern Court during the Nanboku-chō period, invaded Yamato and expelled Junnin, subduing the Ochi clan and others, and in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永和}} (1559) seized the position of provincial protector (Shugo) from Kōfuku-ji. Later, under the Toyotomi regime, Tutsui Junnin reemerged as a warlord in Yamato Province and established a dual system whereby he simultaneously held the status of protector of Yamato Province and acted as the representative of the government-appointed (kanfu) shuto—the temple retainer class—in administering and safeguarding the temple. However, after Junnin's death, Toyotomi Hideyoshi granted Yamato Province to his brother Hidechō, thereby disbanding the existing system, forcing the shuto and the local "kokumin" (the people of the country) to be dissolved; and for Kōfuku-ji, only the original twenty names (as recorded on the official kanfu shuto list) were allowed to serve as temple administrators and security personnel, with a total stipend of 380 koku. == See Also! == * {{ill|Yamato Eikyō no Ran|ja|大和永享の乱}} * {{ill|Warrior Monk|ja|ウォリアーモンク}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "== See Also! ==" to "== See Also =="
revision.text:
'''Shuto''' (衆徒, しゅと), also known as '''Dōshu''' (堂衆, どうしゅ/どうしゅう), originally refers to the class of Buddhist monks who, from the Heian period onward, resided in large temples and, in addition to engaging in scholarly study and ascetic training, performed the practical administrative duties within the temple. They formed the main body of the temple’s "great multitude" ({{ill|Daisū|ja|大衆}}), and in some contexts the term "daisū" is used exclusively to refer to these shuto. In particular, at {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}} in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}}, where the temple also served a protective function ({{ill|Shugo|ja|守護職}}), the warriors of the country (referred to as ''Yamato Bushi'' (大和武士)) were sometimes called shuto.
== Overview == In general, shuto rank below the scholarly monks ({{ill|Gakuryo|ja|学侶}}) and above the itinerant monks ({{ill|Gyōnin|ja|行人}}). They handled practical tasks within the temple such as security, management, and overall operations. Many shuto came from mid-to-lower class aristocratic families, samurai, or local officials ({{ill|Shōkan|ja|荘官}}), and some possessed martial skills; indeed, armed monk soldiers ({{ill|Sōhei|ja|僧兵}}) were largely composed of shuto. (However, at {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}}, the shuto were predominantly drawn from higher-ranking nobles, and in that temple those expelled from the shuto class organized separately as "dōshu.") They played a central role in temple decision-making and in the enforcement of the temple’s authority (through bodies such as the {{ill|Daisū Shingi|ja|大衆僉議}} and {{ill|Kyōso|ja|強訴}}). Later, as monastic hierarchies became more differentiated, scholarly monks ({{ill|Gakuryo|ja|学侶}}) who were of high-ranking court families ({{ill|Kōkei|ja|Kugyō}}) attempted to monopolize key positions and to influence temple management, leading to fierce opposition from the itinerant monks.
== Yamato Warriors == In Yamato Province, where {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}} had strong connections with the {{ill|Sesseki family|ja|摂関家}}, the temple, upon entering the Kamakura period, eventually secured control over Nara and later over the entire Yamato region. Originally, the shuto of this temple were fixed at twenty by the imperial edict (via the {{ill|Taihōkanfu|ja|太政官符}}), serving as assistants to the bettō and the Sangkō. Over time, however, influential temple families ({{ill|Monzeki|ja|門跡}}) such as those affiliated with {{ill|Ichijō-in|ja|一乗院}} and {{ill|Daijō-in|ja|大乗院}} expanded their power by incorporating domestic warriors and local chieftains (often styled as {{ill|Gokenin|ja|御家人}}) into their own retinues, subsequently leading their scholarly monks to assume administrative and security roles within the temple and in the judicial matters ({{ill|Kendan|ja|検断}}) of Nara. Additionally, through the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, the "divine men" ({{ill|Shinjin|ja|神人}}) of Kasuga Shrine, which had become integrated with Kōfuku-ji, were similarly incorporated, and these Kasuga deities came to be known as "kokumin" (国民).
Notable Yamato shuto include those under the patronage of Ichijō-in—such as members of the {{ill|Tutsui Clan|ja|筒井氏}} and the {{ill|Ochi Clan|ja|越智氏}}—and those under Daijō-in—such as members of the {{ill|Toshichi Clan|ja|十市氏}} and the {{ill|Furuichi Clan|ja|古市氏}}. In the Nanboku-chō period, Tōshichi Tōyasu invaded Kōfuku-ji’s estates and established his own domain as a local lord. In response to a request from the bettō of Kōfuku-ji for the suppression of Tōshichi Tōyasu, the 3rd shogun of the Muromachi Bakufu, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|ja|足利義満}}, dispatched commanders such as {{ill|Shiba Yoshishiro|ja|斯波義将}} and {{ill|Toki Yoriyasu|ja|土岐頼康}} to Yamato in 1379 (Eiwa 5). However, when the Bakufu army showed no signs of commencing an expedition against Tōshichi Tōyasu, Yoshimitsu ordered all the generals of the Tōshichi suppression force to return to Kyoto. Consequently, for the first time in history, the protest by the "Jinboku Nyūroku" (the entry of the sacred tree into the capital) failed, dealing a severe blow to temple power. The 6th shogun, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|ja|足利義教}}, intervened in the personnel decisions of the influential temple families within Yamato Province in order to establish Bakufu authority. However, due to conflicts between the factions of Tōdaichū-bō and Ido, the situation devolved into a quagmire. In Tōdaichū-bō, factions including Ochi, Hashio, and Manzai allied with Southern Court elements such as Sawa and Akiyama emerged, while in Ido, the Tutsui and Toshichi factions prevailed; the rivalry between the Tutsui and Ochi factions eventually split Yamato into two, sparking the conflict known as the "Yamato Eikyō no Ran." In August of the 3rd year of the {{ill|Bunmei era|ja|文明}} (1471), forces led by Fuse (Eastern faction) and Ochi Ie Saka (Western faction) clashed—with Fuse supported by Hashio, Narahara, Kubishira, Toshichi, Tutsui, and Maetaka (Fuse Takada), and Ochi reinforced by Manzai, Hatta, Iitaka, Furuichi, Todata, Koizumi, and Tōtakada (Tōma Takada). With influential guardian daimyō also intervening, the long internal conflict, interspersed with the Ōnin War, eventually ushered in a period of warlord rule during the Sengoku period in Yamato Province.
During the Sengoku period, following the death in battle of [[Hatakeyama clan]] retainer [[Kizawa Nagamasa]] in Kawachi Province, the clans led by {{ill|Tutsui Junshō|ja|筒井氏}} and {{ill|Ochi Clan|ja|越智氏}} expanded their influence. For a time they controlled much of the country and advanced toward becoming warlords, but after Junshō’s sudden death and the succession by his young heir, {{ill|Tutsui Junnin|ja|十市遠康}}, the Ochi and Furuichi clans counterattacked, leading to the disintegration of Tutsui control. Seizing the opportunity, Matsunaga Hisahide, under the Southern Court during the Nanboku-chō period, invaded Yamato and expelled Junnin, subduing the Ochi clan and others, and in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永和}} (1559) seized the position of provincial protector (Shugo) from Kōfuku-ji. Later, under the Toyotomi regime, Tutsui Junnin reemerged as a warlord in Yamato Province and established a dual system whereby he simultaneously held the status of protector of Yamato Province and acted as the representative of the government-appointed (kanfu) shuto—the temple retainer class—in administering and safeguarding the temple. However, after Junnin's death, Toyotomi Hideyoshi granted Yamato Province to his brother Hidechō, thereby disbanding the existing system, forcing the shuto and the local "kokumin" (the people of the country) to be dissolved; and for Kōfuku-ji, only the original twenty names (as recorded on the official kanfu shuto list) were allowed to serve as temple administrators and security personnel, with a total stipend of 380 koku. == See Also == * {{ill|Yamato Eikyō no Ran|ja|大和永享の乱}} * {{ill|Warrior Monk|ja|ウォリアーモンク}}

'''Shuto''' (衆徒, しゅと), also known as '''Dōshu''' (堂衆, どうしゅ/どうしゅう), originally refers to the class of Buddhist monks who, from the Heian period onward, resided in large temples and, in addition to engaging in scholarly study and ascetic training, performed the practical administrative duties within the temple. They formed the main body of the temple’s "great multitude" ({{ill|Daisū|ja|大衆}}), and in some contexts the term "daisū" is used exclusively to refer to these shuto. In particular, at {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}} in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}}, where the temple also served a protective function ({{ill|Shugo|ja|守護職}}), the warriors of the country (referred to as ''Yamato Bushi'' (大和武士)) were sometimes called shuto.
== Overview == In general, shuto rank below the scholarly monks ({{ill|Gakuryo|ja|学侶}}) and above the itinerant monks ({{ill|Gyōnin|ja|行人}}). They handled practical tasks within the temple such as security, management, and overall operations. Many shuto came from mid-to-lower class aristocratic families, samurai, or local officials ({{ill|Shōkan|ja|荘官}}), and some possessed martial skills; indeed, armed monk soldiers ({{ill|Sōhei|ja|僧兵}}) were largely composed of shuto. (However, at {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}}, the shuto were predominantly drawn from higher-ranking nobles, and in that temple those expelled from the shuto class organized separately as "dōshu.") They played a central role in temple decision-making and in the enforcement of the temple’s authority (through bodies such as the {{ill|Daisū Shingi|ja|大衆僉議}} and {{ill|Kyōso|ja|強訴}}). Later, as monastic hierarchies became more differentiated, scholarly monks ({{ill|Gakuryo|ja|学侶}}) who were of high-ranking court families ({{ill|Kōkei|ja|Kugyō}}) attempted to monopolize key positions and to influence temple management, leading to fierce opposition from the itinerant monks.
== Yamato Warriors == In Yamato Province, where {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}} had strong connections with the {{ill|Sesseki family|ja|摂関家}}, the temple, upon entering the Kamakura period, eventually secured control over Nara and later over the entire Yamato region. Originally, the shuto of this temple were fixed at twenty by the imperial edict (via the {{ill|Taihōkanfu|ja|太政官符}}), serving as assistants to the bettō and the Sangkō. Over time, however, influential temple families ({{ill|Monzeki|ja|門跡}}) such as those affiliated with {{ill|Ichijō-in|ja|一乗院}} and {{ill|Daijō-in|ja|大乗院}} expanded their power by incorporating domestic warriors and local chieftains (often styled as {{ill|Gokenin|ja|御家人}}) into their own retinues, subsequently leading their scholarly monks to assume administrative and security roles within the temple and in the judicial matters ({{ill|Kendan|ja|検断}}) of Nara. Additionally, through the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, the "divine men" ({{ill|Shinjin|ja|神人}}) of Kasuga Shrine, which had become integrated with Kōfuku-ji, were similarly incorporated, and these Kasuga deities came to be known as "kokumin" (国民).
Notable Yamato shuto include those under the patronage of Ichijō-in—such as members of the {{ill|Tutsui Clan|ja|筒井氏}} and the {{ill|Ochi Clan|ja|越智氏}}—and those under Daijō-in—such as members of the {{ill|Toshichi Clan|ja|十市氏}} and the {{ill|Furuichi Clan|ja|古市氏}}. In the Nanboku-chō period, Tōshichi Tōyasu invaded Kōfuku-ji’s estates and established his own domain as a local lord. In response to a request from the bettō of Kōfuku-ji for the suppression of Tōshichi Tōyasu, the 3rd shogun of the Muromachi Bakufu, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|ja|足利義満}}, dispatched commanders such as {{ill|Shiba Yoshishiro|ja|斯波義将}} and {{ill|Toki Yoriyasu|ja|土岐頼康}} to Yamato in 1379 (Eiwa 5). However, when the Bakufu army showed no signs of commencing an expedition against Tōshichi Tōyasu, Yoshimitsu ordered all the generals of the Tōshichi suppression force to return to Kyoto. Consequently, for the first time in history, the protest by the "Jinboku Nyūroku" (the entry of the sacred tree into the capital) failed, dealing a severe blow to temple power. The 6th shogun, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|ja|足利義教}}, intervened in the personnel decisions of the influential temple families within Yamato Province in order to establish Bakufu authority. However, due to conflicts between the factions of Tōdaichū-bō and Ido, the situation devolved into a quagmire. In Tōdaichū-bō, factions including Ochi, Hashio, and Manzai allied with Southern Court elements such as Sawa and Akiyama emerged, while in Ido, the Tutsui and Toshichi factions prevailed; the rivalry between the Tutsui and Ochi factions eventually split Yamato into two, sparking the conflict known as the "Yamato Eikyō no Ran." In August of the 3rd year of the {{ill|Bunmei era|ja|文明}} (1471), forces led by Fuse (Eastern faction) and Ochi Ie Saka (Western faction) clashed—with Fuse supported by Hashio, Narahara, Kubishira, Toshichi, Tutsui, and Maetaka (Fuse Takada), and Ochi reinforced by Manzai, Hatta, Iitaka, Furuichi, Todata, Koizumi, and Tōtakada (Tōma Takada). With influential guardian daimyō also intervening, the long internal conflict, interspersed with the Ōnin War, eventually ushered in a period of warlord rule during the Sengoku period in Yamato Province.
During the Sengoku period, following the death in battle of [[Hatakeyama clan]] retainer [[Kizawa Nagamasa]] in Kawachi Province, the clans led by {{ill|Tutsui Junshō|ja|筒井氏}} and {{ill|Ochi Clan|ja|越智氏}} expanded their influence. For a time they controlled much of the country and advanced toward becoming warlords, but after Junshō’s sudden death and the succession by his young heir, {{ill|Tutsui Junnin|ja|十市遠康}}, the Ochi and Furuichi clans counterattacked, leading to the disintegration of Tutsui control. Seizing the opportunity, Matsunaga Hisahide, under the Southern Court during the Nanboku-chō period, invaded Yamato and expelled Junnin, subduing the Ochi clan and others, and in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永和}} (1559) seized the position of provincial protector (Shugo) from Kōfuku-ji. Later, under the Toyotomi regime, Tutsui Junnin reemerged as a warlord in Yamato Province and established a dual system whereby he simultaneously held the status of protector of Yamato Province and acted as the representative of the government-appointed (kanfu) shuto—the temple retainer class—in administering and safeguarding the temple. However, after Junnin's death, Toyotomi Hideyoshi granted Yamato Province to his brother Hidechō, thereby disbanding the existing system, forcing the shuto and the local "kokumin" (the people of the country) to be dissolved; and for Kōfuku-ji, only the original twenty names (as recorded on the official kanfu shuto list) were allowed to serve as temple administrators and security personnel, with a total stipend of 380 koku. == See Also == * {{ill|Yamato Eikyō no Ran|ja|大和永享の乱}} * {{ill|Warrior Monk|ja|ウォリアーモンク}}

'''Shuto''' (衆徒, しゅと), also known as '''Dōshu''' (堂衆, どうしゅ/どうしゅう), originally refers to the class of Buddhist monks who, from the Heian period onward, resided in large temples and, in addition to engaging in scholarly study and ascetic training, performed the practical administrative duties within the temple. They formed the main body of the temple’s "great multitude" ({{ill|Daisū|ja|大衆}}), and in some contexts the term "daisū" is used exclusively to refer to these shuto. In particular, at {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}} in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}}, where the temple also served a protective function ({{ill|Shugo|ja|守護職}}), the warriors of the country (referred to as ''Yamato Bushi'' (大和武士)) were sometimes called shuto.
== Overview == In general, shuto rank below the scholarly monks ({{ill|Gakuryo|ja|学侶}}) and above the itinerant monks ({{ill|Gyōnin|ja|行人}}). They handled practical tasks within the temple such as security, management, and overall operations. Many shuto came from mid-to-lower class aristocratic families, samurai, or local officials ({{ill|Shōkan|ja|荘官}}), and some possessed martial skills; indeed, armed monk soldiers ({{ill|Sōhei|ja|僧兵}}) were largely composed of shuto. (However, at {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}}, the shuto were predominantly drawn from higher-ranking nobles, and in that temple those expelled from the shuto class organized separately as "dōshu.") They played a central role in temple decision-making and in the enforcement of the temple’s authority (through bodies such as the {{ill|Daisū Shingi|ja|大衆僉議}} and {{ill|Kyōso|ja|強訴}}). Later, as monastic hierarchies became more differentiated, scholarly monks ({{ill|Gakuryo|ja|学侶}}) who were of high-ranking court families ({{ill|Kōkei|ja|Kugyō}}) attempted to monopolize key positions and to influence temple management, leading to fierce opposition from the itinerant monks.
== Yamato Warriors == In Yamato Province, where {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}} had strong connections with the {{ill|Sesseki family|ja|摂関家}}, the temple, upon entering the Kamakura period, eventually secured control over Nara and later over the entire Yamato region. Originally, the shuto of this temple were fixed at twenty by the imperial edict (via the {{ill|Taihōkanfu|ja|太政官符}}), serving as assistants to the bettō and the Sangkō. Over time, however, influential temple families ({{ill|Monzeki|ja|門跡}}) such as those affiliated with {{ill|Ichijō-in|ja|一乗院}} and {{ill|Daijō-in|ja|大乗院}} expanded their power by incorporating domestic warriors and local chieftains (often styled as {{ill|Gokenin|ja|御家人}}) into their own retinues, subsequently leading their scholarly monks to assume administrative and security roles within the temple and in the judicial matters ({{ill|Kendan|ja|検断}}) of Nara. Additionally, through the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, the "divine men" ({{ill|Shinjin|ja|神人}}) of Kasuga Shrine, which had become integrated with Kōfuku-ji, were similarly incorporated, and these Kasuga deities came to be known as "kokumin" (国民).
Notable Yamato shuto include those under the patronage of Ichijō-in—such as members of the {{ill|Tutsui Clan|ja|筒井氏}} and the {{ill|Ochi Clan|ja|越智氏}}—and those under Daijō-in—such as members of the {{ill|Toshichi Clan|ja|十市氏}} and the {{ill|Furuichi Clan|ja|古市氏}}. In the Nanboku-chō period, Tōshichi Tōyasu invaded Kōfuku-ji’s estates and established his own domain as a local lord. In response to a request from the bettō of Kōfuku-ji for the suppression of Tōshichi Tōyasu, the 3rd shogun of the Muromachi Bakufu, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|ja|足利義満}}, dispatched commanders such as {{ill|Shiba Yoshishiro|ja|斯波義将}} and {{ill|Toki Yoriyasu|ja|土岐頼康}} to Yamato in 1379 (Eiwa 5). However, when the Bakufu army showed no signs of commencing an expedition against Tōshichi Tōyasu, Yoshimitsu ordered all the generals of the Tōshichi suppression force to return to Kyoto. Consequently, for the first time in history, the protest by the "Jinboku Nyūroku" (the entry of the sacred tree into the capital) failed, dealing a severe blow to temple power. The 6th shogun, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|ja|足利義教}}, intervened in the personnel decisions of the influential temple families within Yamato Province in order to establish Bakufu authority. However, due to conflicts between the factions of Tōdaichū-bō and Ido, the situation devolved into a quagmire. In Tōdaichū-bō, factions including Ochi, Hashio, and Manzai allied with Southern Court elements such as Sawa and Akiyama emerged, while in Ido, the Tutsui and Toshichi factions prevailed; the rivalry between the Tutsui and Ochi factions eventually split Yamato into two, sparking the conflict known as the "Yamato Eikyō no Ran." In August of the 3rd year of the {{ill|Bunmei era|ja|文明}} (1471), forces led by Fuse (Eastern faction) and Ochi Ie Saka (Western faction) clashed—with Fuse supported by Hashio, Narahara, Kubishira, Toshichi, Tutsui, and Maetaka (Fuse Takada), and Ochi reinforced by Manzai, Hatta, Iitaka, Furuichi, Todata, Koizumi, and Tōtakada (Tōma Takada). With influential guardian daimyō also intervening, the long internal conflict, interspersed with the Ōnin War, eventually ushered in a period of warlord rule during the Sengoku period in Yamato Province.
During the Sengoku period, following the death in battle of [[Hatakeyama clan]] retainer [[Kizawa Nagamasa]] in Kawachi Province, the clans led by {{ill|Tutsui Junshō|ja|筒井氏}} and {{ill|Ochi Clan|ja|越智氏}} expanded their influence. For a time they controlled much of the country and advanced toward becoming warlords, but after Junshō’s sudden death and the succession by his young heir, {{ill|Tutsui Junnin|ja|十市遠康}}, the Ochi and Furuichi clans counterattacked, leading to the disintegration of Tutsui control. Seizing the opportunity, Matsunaga Hisahide, under the Southern Court during the Nanboku-chō period, invaded Yamato and expelled Junnin, subduing the Ochi clan and others, and in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永和}} (1559) seized the position of provincial protector (Shugo) from Kōfuku-ji. Later, under the Toyotomi regime, Tutsui Junnin reemerged as a warlord in Yamato Province and established a dual system whereby he simultaneously held the status of protector of Yamato Province and acted as the representative of the government-appointed (kanfu) shuto—the temple retainer class—in administering and safeguarding the temple. However, after Junnin's death, Toyotomi Hideyoshi granted Yamato Province to his brother Hidechō, thereby disbanding the existing system, forcing the shuto and the local "kokumin" (the people of the country) to be dissolved; and for Kōfuku-ji, only the original twenty names (as recorded on the official kanfu shuto list) were allowed to serve as temple administrators and security personnel, with a total stipend of 380 koku. == See Also == * {{ill|Yamato Eikyō no Ran|ja|大和永享の乱}} * {{ill|Warrior Monk|ja|ウォリアーモンク}}

revision.text:
'''Shuto''' (衆徒, しゅと), also known as '''Dōshu''' (堂衆, どうしゅ/どうしゅう), originally refers to the class of Buddhist monks who, from the Heian period onward, resided in large temples and, in addition to engaging in scholarly study and ascetic training, performed the practical administrative duties within the temple. They formed the main body of the temple’s "great multitude" ({{ill|Daisū|ja|大衆}}), and in some contexts the term "daisū" is used exclusively to refer to these shuto. In particular, at {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}} in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}}, where the temple also served a protective function ({{ill|Shugo|ja|守護職}}), the warriors of the country (referred to as ''Yamato Bushi'' (大和武士)) were sometimes called shuto.
== Overview == In general, shuto rank below the scholarly monks ({{ill|Gakuryo|ja|学侶}}) and above the itinerant monks ({{ill|Gyōnin|ja|行人}}). They handled practical tasks within the temple such as security, management, and overall operations. Many shuto came from mid-to-lower class aristocratic families, samurai, or local officials ({{ill|Shōkan|ja|荘官}}), and some possessed martial skills; indeed, armed monk soldiers ({{ill|Sōhei|ja|僧兵}}) were largely composed of shuto. (However, at {{ill|Enryaku-ji|ja|延暦寺}}, the shuto were predominantly drawn from higher-ranking nobles, and in that temple those expelled from the shuto class organized separately as "dōshu.") They played a central role in temple decision-making and in the enforcement of the temple’s authority (through bodies such as the {{ill|Daisū Shingi|ja|大衆僉議}} and {{ill|Kyōso|ja|強訴}}). Later, as monastic hierarchies became more differentiated, scholarly monks ({{ill|Gakuryo|ja|学侶}}) who were of high-ranking court families ({{ill|Kōkei|ja|Kugyō}}) attempted to monopolize key positions and to influence temple management, leading to fierce opposition from the itinerant monks.
== Yamato Warriors == In Yamato Province, where {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}} had strong connections with the {{ill|Sesseki family|ja|摂関家}}, the temple, upon entering the Kamakura period, eventually secured control over Nara and later over the entire Yamato region. Originally, the shuto of this temple were fixed at twenty by the imperial edict (via the {{ill|Taihōkanfu|ja|太政官符}}), serving as assistants to the bettō and the Sangkō. Over time, however, influential temple families ({{ill|Monzeki|ja|門跡}}) such as those affiliated with {{ill|Ichijō-in|ja|一乗院}} and {{ill|Daijō-in|ja|大乗院}} expanded their power by incorporating domestic warriors and local chieftains (often styled as {{ill|Gokenin|ja|御家人}}) into their own retinues, subsequently leading their scholarly monks to assume administrative and security roles within the temple and in the judicial matters ({{ill|Kendan|ja|検断}}) of Nara. Additionally, through the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, the "divine men" ({{ill|Shinjin|ja|神人}}) of Kasuga Shrine, which had become integrated with Kōfuku-ji, were similarly incorporated, and these Kasuga deities came to be known as "kokumin" (国民).
Notable Yamato shuto include those under the patronage of Ichijō-in—such as members of the {{ill|Tutsui Clan|ja|筒井氏}} and the {{ill|Ochi Clan|ja|越智氏}}—and those under Daijō-in—such as members of the {{ill|Toshichi Clan|ja|十市氏}} and the {{ill|Furuichi Clan|ja|古市氏}}. In the Nanboku-chō period, Tōshichi Tōyasu invaded Kōfuku-ji’s estates and established his own domain as a local lord. In response to a request from the bettō of Kōfuku-ji for the suppression of Tōshichi Tōyasu, the 3rd shogun of the Muromachi Bakufu, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|ja|足利義満}}, dispatched commanders such as {{ill|Shiba Yoshishiro|ja|斯波義将}} and {{ill|Toki Yoriyasu|ja|土岐頼康}} to Yamato in 1379 (Eiwa 5). However, when the Bakufu army showed no signs of commencing an expedition against Tōshichi Tōyasu, Yoshimitsu ordered all the generals of the Tōshichi suppression force to return to Kyoto. Consequently, for the first time in history, the protest by the "Jinboku Nyūroku" (the entry of the sacred tree into the capital) failed, dealing a severe blow to temple power. The 6th shogun, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|ja|足利義教}}, intervened in the personnel decisions of the influential temple families within Yamato Province in order to establish Bakufu authority. However, due to conflicts between the factions of Tōdaichū-bō and Ido, the situation devolved into a quagmire. In Tōdaichū-bō, factions including Ochi, Hashio, and Manzai allied with Southern Court elements such as Sawa and Akiyama emerged, while in Ido, the Tutsui and Toshichi factions prevailed; the rivalry between the Tutsui and Ochi factions eventually split Yamato into two, sparking the conflict known as the "Yamato Eikyō no Ran." In August of the 3rd year of the {{ill|Bunmei era|ja|文明}} (1471), forces led by Fuse (Eastern faction) and Ochi Ie Saka (Western faction) clashed—with Fuse supported by Hashio, Narahara, Kubishira, Toshichi, Tutsui, and Maetaka (Fuse Takada), and Ochi reinforced by Manzai, Hatta, Iitaka, Furuichi, Todata, Koizumi, and Tōtakada (Tōma Takada). With influential guardian daimyō also intervening, the long internal conflict, interspersed with the Ōnin War, eventually ushered in a period of warlord rule during the Sengoku period in Yamato Province.
During the Sengoku period, following the death in battle of [[Hatakeyama clan]] retainer [[Kizawa Nagamasa]] in Kawachi Province, the clans led by {{ill|Tutsui Junshō|ja|筒井氏}} and {{ill|Ochi Clan|ja|越智氏}} expanded their influence. For a time they controlled much of the country and advanced toward becoming warlords, but after Junshō’s sudden death and the succession by his young heir, {{ill|Tutsui Junnin|ja|十市遠康}}, the Ochi and Furuichi clans counterattacked, leading to the disintegration of Tutsui control. Seizing the opportunity, Matsunaga Hisahide, under the Southern Court during the Nanboku-chō period, invaded Yamato and expelled Junnin, subduing the Ochi clan and others, and in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永和}} (1559) seized the position of provincial protector (Shugo) from Kōfuku-ji. Later, under the Toyotomi regime, Tutsui Junnin reemerged as a warlord in Yamato Province and established a dual system whereby he simultaneously held the status of protector of Yamato Province and acted as the representative of the government-appointed (kanfu) shuto—the temple retainer class—in administering and safeguarding the temple. However, after Junnin's death, Toyotomi Hideyoshi granted Yamato Province to his brother Hidechō, thereby disbanding the existing system, forcing the shuto and the local "kokumin" (the people of the country) to be dissolved; and for Kōfuku-ji, only the original twenty names (as recorded on the official kanfu shuto list) were allowed to serve as temple administrators and security personnel, with a total stipend of 380 koku. == See Also == * {{ill|Yamato Eikyō no Ran|ja|大和永享の乱}} * {{ill|Warrior Monk|ja|ウォリアーモンク}}

revision.text:
'''衆徒'''（しゅと）は、'''堂衆'''（どうしゅ/どうしゅう）とも呼ばれ、本来は[[平安時代]]以後に大寺院に居住して学問・修行の他に寺内の運営実務にあたった[[僧侶]]身分のこと。[[大衆_(仏教)|大衆]]の主力であり、衆徒のみを指して「大衆」と呼ぶ場合もある。特に[[興福寺]]が[[守護|守護職]]を務めた[[大和国]]では、国内の武士を自寺の衆徒として組み入れたために同国の武士（'''大和武士'''（やまとぶし））を指して衆徒と呼ぶ場合もある。
== 概説 == 一般的に[[学侶]]よりも下位、[[行人_(仏教)|行人]]よりも上位とされ、寺院内の警備や管理運営などの実務なども扱った。中下級の[[貴族]]や[[武家]]・[[荘官]]などの出身者が多かったため、武術などの心得を持つものもあり、武装化して[[僧兵]]を構成したのもこの身分が中心であった（ただし、延暦寺では衆徒をより上級貴族出身が占め、このクラスや衆徒から放逐された者達が別に「堂衆」を組織するなど、寺院ごとに組織のあり方に違いがあった）。大衆僉議や[[強訴]]など寺院内部の意思決定やその実力行使に中心的な役割を果たした。後に僧侶の身分分化が進み、[[公卿]]などの出身である学侶が寺院内の要職を独占して寺院の経営にも関与しようとすると、行人とともに激しく抵抗した。
== 大和武士 == [[摂関家]]とのつながりが強かった大和国の興福寺は[[鎌倉時代]]に入ると、南都[[奈良]]やがて大和一国の支配権を得るようになった。本来同寺の衆徒は[[太政官符]]によって20名と定められ、[[別当]]や[[三綱]]の補佐にあたることになっていた（官符衆徒）が、次第に[[一乗院]]・[[大乗院 (門跡寺院)|大乗院]]などといった有力な[[門跡]]が自己の発言力の増大のために国内の武士や名主などを[[御家人]]などと称して自己の衆徒に組み入れ、自院の学侶に率いさせて寺内や奈良の町の[[検断]]などに従事させた。また、[[神仏習合]]によって興福寺と一体化していた[[春日社]]の[[神人]]に組み入れられて同様の役割を果たすこともあり、こうした春日社神人を「'''国民'''」と呼んだ。
代表的な大和の衆徒として一乗院傘下の[[筒井氏]]・[[越智氏 (大和国)|越智氏]]、大乗院傘下の[[十市氏]]・[[古市氏]]などがいる。[[南北朝時代_(日本)|南北朝期]]になると[[十市遠康]]が、興福寺の所領を侵し、自らの支配地域を獲得して[[領主]]化する。興福寺別当から十市遠康討伐の要請を受け[[室町幕府]]第3代[[征夷大将軍|将軍]][[足利義満]]は永和五年[[1379年]]、[[斯波義将]]、[[土岐頼康]]らを大和に派遣するが、幕府軍は十市遠康討伐を開始する気配を見せず、義満は十市氏討伐軍の諸将に、京への帰還を命じる。これにより歴史上初めて神木入洛による強訴を失敗に終わらせて[[寺社勢力]]に大打撃を与えてしまった。第6代将軍[[足利義教]]は、大和国内に幕府の支配権を確立するために両門跡の人事に介入した。しかし、豊田中坊と井戸とのあいだでおこった紛争に幕府がまきこまれ、事態は泥沼化してしまう。豊田中坊には、越智・箸尾・万歳と後南朝方の沢・秋山がつき、井戸は、筒井・十市がささえ、筒井対越智の対立は大和を二分する状況になり[[大和永享の乱]]と呼ばれる争いがはじまる。[[文明 (日本)|文明]]3(1471)年8月には布施（東軍）と越智家栄（西軍）が闘うが、布施には箸尾・楢原・倶尸羅・十市・筒井・前高田（布施高田）、越智には万歳・八田・飯高・古市・吐田・小泉・当高田（当麻高田）が援軍している。そこに有力[[守護大名]]も介入して[[応仁の乱]]を挟んだ長期の内紛へと発展した。応仁の乱では東軍の[[筒井順永]]・[[十市遠清]]と西軍の[[越智家栄]]・[[古市澄胤]]が激しく争い、以後大和国内は彼らの子孫たちによって群雄割拠された状態で[[戦国時代_(日本)|戦国時代]]を迎えることになる。
戦国時代には[[畠山氏]]の重臣[[木沢長政]]が[[河内国]]で討死するとそれに乗じて[[筒井順昭]]と[[十市遠忠]]が勢力を拡大した。一時国内の大半を平定して[[戦国大名]]化を進めるが、順昭が急死して幼少の[[筒井順慶|順慶]]が後を継ぐと、越智氏と古市氏が反撃して筒井氏の支配は解体、更にその間隙を縫って[[三好長慶]]配下の[[松永久秀]]が大和に攻め込んで順慶を追放、越智氏らを屈服させて[[永禄]]2年（[[1559年]]）興福寺から大和国守護職の地位を奪った。その後、[[織豊政権]]のもとで大和一国の大名として復活した筒井順慶は、対外的には大和国主として国内的には父祖代々継承してきた官符衆徒の代表として[[法印]][[僧都]]の地位に就くという二面性を持った支配体制を確立する。だが、順慶の死後には[[豊臣秀吉]]が弟の[[豊臣秀長|秀長]]に大和一国を与えて、既存の支配体制を否定、衆徒・国民を強制的に解散させ、興福寺に対しては官符衆徒本来の20氏（名）のみを寺内の事務・警備要員として採用して総計380石を与えることを認めたのである。
== 関連項目 == *[[大和永享の乱]] *[[ウォリアーモンク]] [[category:僧|しゆと]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[衆徒]] to [[Shuto]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:衆徒]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11625971]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shuto''' (衆徒, しゅと), also known as '''Dōshu''' (堂衆, どうしゅ/どうしゅう), originally refers to the class of Buddhist monks who, from the Heian period onward, resided in large temples and, in addition to engaging in scholarly study and ascetic training, performed the practical administrative duties within the temple. They formed the main body of the temple’s "great multitude" ({{ill|Daisū|ja|大衆|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisū|qq=|1=general public|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5369389}}), and in some contexts the term "daisū" is used exclusively to refer to these shuto. In particular, at [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] in [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]], where the temple also served a protective function ({{ill|Shugo|ja|守護職|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 守護|1=shugo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1141023}}), the warriors of the country (referred to as ''Yamato Bushi'' (大和武士)) were sometimes called shuto.
== Overview == In general, shuto Rank below the scholarly monks ({{ill|Gakuryo|ja|学侶|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gakuryo|qq=|1=Gakuryo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448122}}) and above the itinerant monks ([[:en:The Wayfarer (novel)|Gyōnin]]). They handled practical tasks within the temple such as security, management, and overall operations. Many shuto came from mid-to-lower class aristocratic families, samurai, or local officials ({{ill|Shōkan|ja|荘官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkan|qq=|1=shōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618914}}), and some possessed martial skills; indeed, armed monk soldiers ([[:en:Sōhei|Sōhei]]) were largely composed of shuto. (However, at [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]], the shuto were predominantly drawn from higher-Ranking nobles, and in that temple those expelled from the shuto class organized separately as "dōshu.") They played a central role in temple decision-making and in the enforcement of the temple’s authority (through bodies such as the {{ill|Daisū Shingi|ja|大衆僉議|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisū Shingi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Kyōso|ja|強訴|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōso|qq=|1=gōso|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487759}}). Later, as monastic hierarchies became more differentiated, scholarly monks ({{ill|Gakuryo|ja|学侶|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gakuryo|qq=|1=Gakuryo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448122}}) who were of high-Ranking court families ({{ill|Kōkei|ja|Kugyō|1=kugyō|2=ja|3=公卿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōkei|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3138036}}) attempted to monopolize key positions and to influence temple management, leading to fierce opposition from the itinerant monks.
== Yamato Warriors == In Yamato Province, where [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] had strong connections with the [[:en:Five regent houses|Five regent houses]], the temple, upon entering the Kamakura period, eventually secured control over Nara and later over the entire Yamato region. Originally, the shuto of this temple were fixed at twenty by the imperial edict (via the {{ill|Taihōkanfu|ja|太政官符|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taihōkanfu|qq=|1=Daijō-kan-pu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444013}}), serving as assistants to the bettō and the Sangkō. Over time, however, influential temple families ([[:en:Monzeki|Monzeki]]) such as those affiliated with {{ill|Ichijō-in|ja|一乗院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichijō-in|qq=|1=Ichijō-in|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11352379}} and {{ill|Daijō-in|ja|大乗院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijō-in|qq=|1=Daijō-in|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16766994}} expanded their power by incorporating domestic warriors and local chieftains (often styled as [[:en:Gokenin|Gokenin]]) into their own retinues, subsequently leading their scholarly monks to assume administrative and security roles within the temple and in the judicial matters ({{ill|Kendan|ja|検断|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kendan|qq=|1=Kendan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540935}}) of Nara. Additionally, through the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, the "divine men" ({{ill|Shinjin|ja|神人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinjin|qq=|1=jinin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588676}}) of Kasuga Shrine, which had become integrated with Kōfuku-ji, were similarly incorporated, and these Kasuga deities came to be known as "kokumin" (国民).
Notable Yamato shuto include those under the patronage of Ichijō-in—such as members of the [[:en:Tsutsui clan|Tutsui Clan]] and the {{ill|Ochi Clan|ja|越智氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ochi Clan|qq=|1=Ochi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636555}}—and those under Daijō-in—such as members of the {{ill|Toshichi Clan|ja|十市氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshichi Clan|qq=|1=Toichi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109600528}} and the {{ill|Furuichi Clan|ja|古市氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furuichi Clan|qq=|1=Furuichi Clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109595566}}. In the Nanboku-chō period, Tōshichi Tōyasu invaded Kōfuku-ji’s estates and established his own domain as a local lord. In response to a request from the bettō of Kōfuku-ji for the suppression of Tōshichi Tōyasu, the 3rd shogun of the Muromachi Bakufu, [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu]], dispatched commanders such as [[:en:Shiba Yoshiyuki|Shiba Yoshishiro]] and {{ill|Toki Yoriyasu|ja|土岐頼康|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toki Yoriyasu|qq=|1=Yoriyasu Toki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423410}} to Yamato in 1379 (Eiwa 5). However, when the Bakufu army showed no signs of commencing an expedition against Tōshichi Tōyasu, Yoshimitsu ordered all the generals of the Tōshichi suppression force to return to Kyoto. Consequently, for the first time in history, the protest by the "Jinboku Nyūroku" (the entry of the sacred tree into the capital) failed, dealing a severe blow to temple power. The 6th shogun, [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshinori|Ashikaga Yoshinori]], intervened in the personnel decisions of the influential temple families within Yamato Province in order to establish Bakufu authority. However, due to conflicts between the factions of Tōdaichū-bō and Ido, the situation devolved into a quagmire. In Tōdaichū-bō, factions including Ochi, Hashio, and Manzai allied with Southern Court elements such as Sawa and Akiyama emerged, while in Ido, the Tutsui and Toshichi factions prevailed; the rivalry between the Tutsui and Ochi factions eventually split Yamato into two, sparking the conflict known as the "Yamato Eikyō no Ran." In August of the 3rd year of the [[:en:Civilization|Bunmei era]] (1471), forces led by Fuse (Eastern faction) and Ochi Ie Saka (Western faction) clashed—with Fuse supported by Hashio, Narahara, Kubishira, Toshichi, Tutsui, and Maetaka (Fuse Takada), and Ochi reinforced by Manzai, Hatta, Iitaka, Furuichi, Todata, Koizumi, and Tōtakada (Tōma Takada). With influential guardian daimyō also intervening, the long internal conflict, interspersed with the Ōnin War, eventually ushered in a period of warlord rule during the Sengoku period in Yamato Province.
During the Sengoku period, following the death in battle of [[:en:Hatakeyama clan|Hatakeyama clan]] retainer [[:en:Kizawa Nagamasa|Kizawa Nagamasa]] in Kawachi Province, the clans led by [[:en:Tsutsui clan|Tutsui Junshō]] and {{ill|Ochi Clan|ja|越智氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ochi Clan|qq=|1=Ochi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636555}} expanded their influence. For a time they controlled much of the country and advanced toward becoming warlords, but after Junshō’s sudden death and the succession by his young heir, {{ill|Tutsui Junnin|ja|十市遠康|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tutsui Junnin|qq=|1=Tōchi Tōyasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11405031}}, the Ochi and Furuichi clans counterattacked, leading to the disintegration of Tutsui control. Seizing the opportunity, Matsunaga Hisahide, under the Southern Court during the Nanboku-chō period, invaded Yamato and expelled Junnin, subduing the Ochi clan and others, and in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永和|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eiroku era|qq=|1=Eiwa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11131293}} (1559) seized the position of provincial protector (Shugo) from Kōfuku-ji. Later, under the Toyotomi regime, Tutsui Junnin reemerged as a warlord in Yamato Province and established a dual system whereby he simultaneously held the status of protector of Yamato Province and acted as the representative of the government-appointed (kanfu) shuto—the temple retainer class—in administering and safeguarding the temple. However, after Junnin's death, Toyotomi Hideyoshi granted Yamato Province to his brother Hidechō, thereby disbanding the existing system, forcing the shuto and the local "kokumin" (the people of the country) to be dissolved; and for Kōfuku-ji, only the original twenty names (as recorded on the official kanfu shuto list) were allowed to serve as temple administrators and security personnel, with a total stipend of 380 koku. == See Also == * {{ill|Yamato Eikyō no Ran|ja|大和永享の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Eikyō no Ran|qq=|1=Yamato Eikyō War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433497}} * [[:en:Warrior monk|Warrior Monk]]

[[ja:寺社勢力]] [[en:Shuto]] [[Category:Monks]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[en:Shuto]] {{wikidata link|Q11625971}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:衆徒]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11625971]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shuto''' (衆徒, しゅと), also known as '''Dōshu''' (堂衆, どうしゅ/どうしゅう), originally refers to the class of Buddhist monks who, from the Heian period onward, resided in large temples and, in addition to engaging in scholarly study and ascetic training, performed the practical administrative duties within the temple. They formed the main body of the temple’s "great multitude" ({{ill|Daisū|ja|大衆|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisū|qq=|1=general public|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5369389}}), and in some contexts the term "daisū" is used exclusively to refer to these shuto. In particular, at [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] in [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]], where the temple also served a protective function ({{ill|Shugo|ja|守護職|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 守護|1=shugo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1141023}}), the warriors of the country (referred to as ''Yamato Bushi'' (大和武士)) were sometimes called shuto.
== Overview == In general, shuto Rank below the scholarly monks ({{ill|Gakuryo|ja|学侶|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gakuryo|qq=|1=Gakuryo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448122}}) and above the itinerant monks ([[:en:The Wayfarer (novel)|Gyōnin]]). They handled practical tasks within the temple such as security, management, and overall operations. Many shuto came from mid-to-lower class aristocratic families, samurai, or local officials ({{ill|Shōkan|ja|荘官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkan|qq=|1=shōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618914}}), and some possessed martial skills; indeed, armed monk soldiers ([[:en:Sōhei|Sōhei]]) were largely composed of shuto. (However, at [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]], the shuto were predominantly drawn from higher-Ranking nobles, and in that temple those expelled from the shuto class organized separately as "dōshu.") They played a central role in temple decision-making and in the enforcement of the temple’s authority (through bodies such as the {{ill|Daisū Shingi|ja|大衆僉議|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisū Shingi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Kyōso|ja|強訴|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōso|qq=|1=gōso|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487759}}). Later, as monastic hierarchies became more differentiated, scholarly monks ({{ill|Gakuryo|ja|学侶|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gakuryo|qq=|1=Gakuryo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448122}}) who were of high-Ranking court families ({{ill|Kōkei|ja|Kugyō|1=kugyō|2=ja|3=公卿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōkei|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3138036}}) attempted to monopolize key positions and to influence temple management, leading to fierce opposition from the itinerant monks.
== Yamato Warriors == In Yamato Province, where [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] had strong connections with the [[:en:Five regent houses|Five regent houses]], the temple, upon entering the Kamakura period, eventually secured control over Nara and later over the entire Yamato region. Originally, the shuto of this temple were fixed at twenty by the imperial edict (via the {{ill|Taihōkanfu|ja|太政官符|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taihōkanfu|qq=|1=Daijō-kan-pu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444013}}), serving as assistants to the bettō and the Sangkō. Over time, however, influential temple families ([[:en:Monzeki|Monzeki]]) such as those affiliated with {{ill|Ichijō-in|ja|一乗院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichijō-in|qq=|1=Ichijō-in|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11352379}} and {{ill|Daijō-in|ja|大乗院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijō-in|qq=|1=Daijō-in|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16766994}} expanded their power by incorporating domestic warriors and local chieftains (often styled as [[:en:Gokenin|Gokenin]]) into their own retinues, subsequently leading their scholarly monks to assume administrative and security roles within the temple and in the judicial matters ({{ill|Kendan|ja|検断|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kendan|qq=|1=Kendan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540935}}) of Nara. Additionally, through the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, the "divine men" ({{ill|Shinjin|ja|神人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinjin|qq=|1=jinin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588676}}) of Kasuga Shrine, which had become integrated with Kōfuku-ji, were similarly incorporated, and these Kasuga deities came to be known as "kokumin" (国民).
Notable Yamato shuto include those under the patronage of Ichijō-in—such as members of the [[:en:Tsutsui clan|Tutsui Clan]] and the {{ill|Ochi Clan|ja|越智氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ochi Clan|qq=|1=Ochi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636555}}—and those under Daijō-in—such as members of the {{ill|Toshichi Clan|ja|十市氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshichi Clan|qq=|1=Toichi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109600528}} and the {{ill|Furuichi Clan|ja|古市氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furuichi Clan|qq=|1=Furuichi Clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109595566}}. In the Nanboku-chō period, Tōshichi Tōyasu invaded Kōfuku-ji’s estates and established his own domain as a local lord. In response to a request from the bettō of Kōfuku-ji for the suppression of Tōshichi Tōyasu, the 3rd shogun of the Muromachi Bakufu, [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu]], dispatched commanders such as [[:en:Shiba Yoshiyuki|Shiba Yoshishiro]] and {{ill|Toki Yoriyasu|ja|土岐頼康|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toki Yoriyasu|qq=|1=Yoriyasu Toki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423410}} to Yamato in 1379 (Eiwa 5). However, when the Bakufu army showed no signs of commencing an expedition against Tōshichi Tōyasu, Yoshimitsu ordered all the generals of the Tōshichi suppression force to return to Kyoto. Consequently, for the first time in history, the protest by the "Jinboku Nyūroku" (the entry of the sacred tree into the capital) failed, dealing a severe blow to temple power. The 6th shogun, [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshinori|Ashikaga Yoshinori]], intervened in the personnel decisions of the influential temple families within Yamato Province in order to establish Bakufu authority. However, due to conflicts between the factions of Tōdaichū-bō and Ido, the situation devolved into a quagmire. In Tōdaichū-bō, factions including Ochi, Hashio, and Manzai allied with Southern Court elements such as Sawa and Akiyama emerged, while in Ido, the Tutsui and Toshichi factions prevailed; the rivalry between the Tutsui and Ochi factions eventually split Yamato into two, sparking the conflict known as the "Yamato Eikyō no Ran." In August of the 3rd year of the [[:en:Civilization|Bunmei era]] (1471), forces led by Fuse (Eastern faction) and Ochi Ie Saka (Western faction) clashed—with Fuse supported by Hashio, Narahara, Kubishira, Toshichi, Tutsui, and Maetaka (Fuse Takada), and Ochi reinforced by Manzai, Hatta, Iitaka, Furuichi, Todata, Koizumi, and Tōtakada (Tōma Takada). With influential guardian daimyō also intervening, the long internal conflict, interspersed with the Ōnin War, eventually ushered in a period of warlord rule during the Sengoku period in Yamato Province.
During the Sengoku period, following the death in battle of [[:en:Hatakeyama clan|Hatakeyama clan]] retainer [[:en:Kizawa Nagamasa|Kizawa Nagamasa]] in Kawachi Province, the clans led by [[:en:Tsutsui clan|Tutsui Junshō]] and {{ill|Ochi Clan|ja|越智氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ochi Clan|qq=|1=Ochi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636555}} expanded their influence. For a time they controlled much of the country and advanced toward becoming warlords, but after Junshō’s sudden death and the succession by his young heir, {{ill|Tutsui Junnin|ja|十市遠康|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tutsui Junnin|qq=|1=Tōchi Tōyasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11405031}}, the Ochi and Furuichi clans counterattacked, leading to the disintegration of Tutsui control. Seizing the opportunity, Matsunaga Hisahide, under the Southern Court during the Nanboku-chō period, invaded Yamato and expelled Junnin, subduing the Ochi clan and others, and in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永和|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eiroku era|qq=|1=Eiwa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11131293}} (1559) seized the position of provincial protector (Shugo) from Kōfuku-ji. Later, under the Toyotomi regime, Tutsui Junnin reemerged as a warlord in Yamato Province and established a dual system whereby he simultaneously held the status of protector of Yamato Province and acted as the representative of the government-appointed (kanfu) shuto—the temple retainer class—in administering and safeguarding the temple. However, after Junnin's death, Toyotomi Hideyoshi granted Yamato Province to his brother Hidechō, thereby disbanding the existing system, forcing the shuto and the local "kokumin" (the people of the country) to be dissolved; and for Kōfuku-ji, only the original twenty names (as recorded on the official kanfu shuto list) were allowed to serve as temple administrators and security personnel, with a total stipend of 380 koku. == See Also == * {{ill|Yamato Eikyō no Ran|ja|大和永享の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Eikyō no Ran|qq=|1=Yamato Eikyō War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433497}} * [[:en:Warrior monk|Warrior Monk]]

(However, at [[:en:Enryaku-ji|Enryaku-ji]], the shuto were predominantly drawn from higher-Ranking nobles, and in that temple those expelled from the shuto class organized separately as "dōshu.") They played a central role in temple decision-making and in the enforcement of the temple’s authority (through bodies such as the {{ill|Daisū Shingi|ja|大衆僉議|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisū Shingi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Kyōso|ja|強訴|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōso|1=gōso|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487759}}). Later, as monastic hierarchies became more differentiated, scholarly monks ({{ill|Gakuryo|ja|学侶|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gakuryo|1=Gakuryo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448122}}) who were of high-Ranking court families ({{ill|Kōkei|ja|Kugyō|1=kugyō|2=ja|3=公卿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōkei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3138036}}) attempted to monopolize key positions and to influence temple management, leading to fierce opposition from the itinerant monks.
== Yamato Warriors == In Yamato Province, where [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]] had strong connections with the [[:en:Five regent houses|Five regent houses]], the temple, upon entering the Kamakura period, eventually secured control over Nara and later over the entire Yamato region. Originally, the shuto of this temple were fixed at twenty by the imperial edict (via the {{ill|Taihōkanfu|ja|太政官符|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taihōkanfu|1=Daijō-kan-pu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444013}}), serving as assistants to the bettō and the Sangkō. Over time, however, influential temple families ([[:en:Monzeki|Monzeki]]) such as those affiliated with {{ill|Ichijō-in|ja|一乗院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichijō-in|1=Ichijō-in|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11352379}} and {{ill|Daijō-in|ja|大乗院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijō-in|1=Daijō-in|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16766994}} expanded their power by incorporating domestic warriors and local chieftains (often styled as [[:en:Gokenin|Gokenin]]) into their own retinues, subsequently leading their scholarly monks to assume administrative and security roles within the temple and in the judicial matters ({{ill|Kendan|ja|検断|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kendan|1=Kendan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540935}}) of Nara. Additionally, through the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, the "divine men" ({{ill|Shinjin|ja|神人|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinjin|1=jinin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588676}}) of Kasuga Shrine, which had become integrated with Kōfuku-ji, were similarly incorporated, and these Kasuga deities came to be known as "kokumin" (国民).
Notable Yamato shuto include those under the patronage of Ichijō-in—such as members of the [[:en:Tsutsui clan|Tutsui Clan]] and the {{ill|Ochi Clan|ja|越智氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ochi Clan|1=Ochi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636555}}—and those under Daijō-in—such as members of the {{ill|Toshichi Clan|ja|十市氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshichi Clan|1=Toichi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109600528}} and the {{ill|Furuichi Clan|ja|古市氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furuichi Clan|1=Furuichi Clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109595566}}. In the Nanboku-chō period, Tōshichi Tōyasu invaded Kōfuku-ji’s estates and established his own domain as a local lord. In response to a request from the bettō of Kōfuku-ji for the suppression of Tōshichi Tōyasu, the 3rd shogun of the Muromachi Bakufu, [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu]], dispatched commanders such as [[:en:Shiba Yoshiyuki|Shiba Yoshishiro]] and {{ill|Toki Yoriyasu|ja|土岐頼康|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toki Yoriyasu|1=Yoriyasu Toki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423410}} to Yamato in 1379 (Eiwa 5). However, when the Bakufu army showed no signs of commencing an expedition against Tōshichi Tōyasu, Yoshimitsu ordered all the generals of the Tōshichi suppression force to return to Kyoto. Consequently, for the first time in history, the protest by the "Jinboku Nyūroku" (the entry of the sacred tree into the capital) failed, dealing a severe blow to temple power. The 6th shogun, [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshinori|Ashikaga Yoshinori]], intervened in the personnel decisions of the influential temple families within Yamato Province in order to establish Bakufu authority. However, due to conflicts between the factions of Tōdaichū-bō and Ido, the situation devolved into a quagmire. In Tōdaichū-bō, factions including Ochi, Hashio, and Manzai allied with Southern Court elements such as Sawa and Akiyama emerged, while in Ido, the Tutsui and Toshichi factions prevailed; the rivalry between the Tutsui and Ochi factions eventually split Yamato into two, sparking the conflict known as the "Yamato Eikyō no Ran." In August of the 3rd year of the [[:en:Civilization|Bunmei era]] (1471), forces led by Fuse (Eastern faction) and Ochi Ie Saka (Western faction) clashed—with Fuse supported by Hashio, Narahara, Kubishira, Toshichi, Tutsui, and Maetaka (Fuse Takada), and Ochi reinforced by Manzai, Hatta, Iitaka, Furuichi, Todata, Koizumi, and Tōtakada (Tōma Takada). With influential guardian daimyō also intervening, the long internal conflict, interspersed with the Ōnin War, eventually ushered in a period of warlord rule during the Sengoku period in Yamato Province.
During the Sengoku period, following the death in battle of [[:en:Hatakeyama clan|Hatakeyama clan]] retainer [[:en:Kizawa Nagamasa|Kizawa Nagamasa]] in Kawachi Province, the clans led by [[:en:Tsutsui clan|Tutsui Junshō]] and {{ill|Ochi Clan|ja|越智氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ochi Clan|1=Ochi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636555}} expanded their influence. For a time they controlled much of the country and advanced toward becoming warlords, but after Junshō’s sudden death and the succession by his young heir, {{ill|Tutsui Junnin|ja|十市遠康|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tutsui Junnin|1=Tōchi Tōyasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11405031}}, the Ochi and Furuichi clans counterattacked, leading to the disintegration of Tutsui control. Seizing the opportunity, Matsunaga Hisahide, under the Southern Court during the Nanboku-chō period, invaded Yamato and expelled Junnin, subduing the Ochi clan and others, and in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永和|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eiroku era|1=Eiwa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11131293}} (1559) seized the position of provincial protector (Shugo) from Kōfuku-ji. Later, under the Toyotomi regime, Tutsui Junnin reemerged as a warlord in Yamato Province and established a dual system whereby he simultaneously held the status of protector of Yamato Province and acted as the representative of the government-appointed (kanfu) shuto—the temple retainer class—in administering and safeguarding the temple. However, after Junnin's death, Toyotomi Hideyoshi granted Yamato Province to his brother Hidechō, thereby disbanding the existing system, forcing the shuto and the local "kokumin" (the people of the country) to be dissolved; and for Kōfuku-ji, only the original twenty names (as recorded on the official kanfu shuto list) were allowed to serve as temple administrators and security personnel, with a total stipend of 380 koku. == See Also == * {{ill|Yamato Eikyō no Ran|ja|大和永享の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Eikyō no Ran|1=Yamato Eikyō War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433497}} * [[:en:Warrior monk|Warrior Monk]]
