They handled practical tasks within the temple such as security, management, and overall operations. Many shuto came from mid-to-lower class aristocratic families, samurai, or local officials ({{ill|Shōkan|ja|荘官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkan|1=shōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618914}}), and some possessed martial skills; indeed, armed monk soldiers ({{ill|Sōhei|en|Sōhei|WD=Q1475695|lt=Sōhei}}) were largely composed of shuto. (However, at {{ill|Enryaku-ji|en|Enryaku-ji|WD=Q917195|lt=Enryaku-ji}}, the shuto were predominantly drawn from higher-Ranking nobles, and in that temple those expelled from the shuto class organized separately as "dōshu.") They played a central role in temple decision-making and in the enforcement of the temple’s authority (through bodies such as the {{ill|Daisū Shingi|ja|大衆僉議|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisū Shingi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Kyōso|ja|強訴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōso|1=gōso|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487759}}). Later, as monastic hierarchies became more differentiated, scholarly monks ({{ill|Gakuryo|ja|学侶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gakuryo|1=Gakuryo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448122}}) who were of high-Ranking court families ({{ill|Kōkei|ja|Kugyō|1=kugyō|2=ja|3=公卿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōkei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3138036}}) attempted to monopolize key positions and to influence temple management, leading to fierce opposition from the itinerant monks.
== Yamato Warriors == In Yamato Province, where {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|en|Kōfuku-ji|WD=Q1070863|lt=Kōfuku-ji}} had strong connections with the {{ill|Five regent houses|en|Five regent houses|WD=Q1067142|lt=Five regent houses}}, the temple, upon entering the Kamakura period, eventually secured control over Nara and later over the entire Yamato region. Originally, the shuto of this temple were fixed at twenty by the imperial edict (via the {{ill|Taihōkanfu|ja|太政官符|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taihōkanfu|1=Daijō-kan-pu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444013}}), serving as assistants to the bettō and the Sangkō. Over time, however, influential temple families ({{ill|Monzeki|en|Monzeki|WD=Q1079855|lt=Monzeki}}) such as those affiliated with {{ill|Ichijō-in|ja|一乗院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichijō-in|1=Ichijō-in|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11352379}} and {{ill|Daijō-in|ja|大乗院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijō-in|1=Daijō-in|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16766994}} expanded their power by incorporating domestic warriors and local chieftains (often styled as {{ill|Gokenin|en|Gokenin|WD=Q63765|lt=Gokenin}}) into their own retinues, subsequently leading their scholarly monks to assume administrative and security roles within the temple and in the judicial matters ({{ill|Kendan|ja|検断|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kendan|1=Kendan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540935}}) of Nara. Additionally, through the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, the "divine men" ({{ill|Shinjin|ja|神人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinjin|1=jinin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588676}}) of Kasuga Shrine, which had become integrated with Kōfuku-ji, were similarly incorporated, and these Kasuga deities came to be known as "kokumin" (国民).
Notable Yamato shuto include those under the patronage of Ichijō-in—such as members of the {{ill|Tsutsui clan|en|Tsutsui clan|WD=Q776477|lt=Tutsui Clan}} and the {{ill|Ochi Clan|ja|越智氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ochi Clan|1=Ochi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636555}}—and those under Daijō-in—such as members of the {{ill|Toshichi Clan|ja|十市氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshichi Clan|1=Toichi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109600528}} and the {{ill|Furuichi Clan|ja|古市氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furuichi Clan|1=Furuichi Clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109595566}}. In the Nanboku-chō period, Tōshichi Tōyasu invaded Kōfuku-ji’s estates and established his own domain as a local lord. In response to a request from the bettō of Kōfuku-ji for the suppression of Tōshichi Tōyasu, the 3rd shogun of the Muromachi Bakufu, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|en|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|WD=Q248381|lt=Ashikaga Yoshimitsu}}, dispatched commanders such as {{ill|Shiba Yoshiyuki|en|Shiba Yoshiyuki|WD=Q11500863|lt=Shiba Yoshishiro}} and {{ill|Toki Yoriyasu|ja|土岐頼康|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toki Yoriyasu|1=Yoriyasu Toki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423410}} to Yamato in 1379 (Eiwa 5). However, when the Bakufu army showed no signs of commencing an expedition against Tōshichi Tōyasu, Yoshimitsu ordered all the generals of the Tōshichi suppression force to return to Kyoto. Consequently, for the first time in history, the protest by the "Jinboku Nyūroku" (the entry of the sacred tree into the capital) failed, dealing a severe blow to temple power. The 6th shogun, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|en|Ashikaga Yoshinori|WD=Q239170|lt=Ashikaga Yoshinori}}, intervened in the personnel decisions of the influential temple families within Yamato Province in order to establish Bakufu authority. However, due to conflicts between the factions of Tōdaichū-bō and Ido, the situation devolved into a quagmire. In Tōdaichū-bō, factions including Ochi, Hashio, and Manzai allied with Southern Court elements such as Sawa and Akiyama emerged, while in Ido, the Tutsui and Toshichi factions prevailed; the rivalry between the Tutsui and Ochi factions eventually split Yamato into two, sparking the conflict known as the "Yamato Eikyō no Ran." In August of the 3rd year of the {{ill|Civilization|en|Civilization|WD=Q8432|lt=Bunmei era}} (1471), forces led by Fuse (Eastern faction) and Ochi Ie Saka (Western faction) clashed—with Fuse supported by Hashio, Narahara, Kubishira, Toshichi, Tutsui, and Maetaka (Fuse Takada), and Ochi reinforced by Manzai, Hatta, Iitaka, Furuichi, Todata, Koizumi, and Tōtakada (Tōma Takada). With influential guardian daimyō also intervening, the long internal conflict, interspersed with the Ōnin War, eventually ushered in a period of warlord rule during the Sengoku period in Yamato Province.
During the Sengoku period, following the death in battle of {{ill|Hatakeyama clan|en|Hatakeyama clan|WD=Q662881|lt=Hatakeyama clan}} retainer {{ill|Kizawa Nagamasa|en|Kizawa Nagamasa|WD=Q6419175|lt=Kizawa Nagamasa}} in Kawachi Province, the clans led by {{ill|Tsutsui clan|en|Tsutsui clan|WD=Q776477|lt=Tutsui Junshō}} and {{ill|Ochi Clan|ja|越智氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ochi Clan|1=Ochi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636555}} expanded their influence. For a time they controlled much of the country and advanced toward becoming warlords, but after Junshō’s sudden death and the succession by his young heir, {{ill|Tutsui Junnin|ja|十市遠康|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tutsui Junnin|1=Tōchi Tōyasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11405031}}, the Ochi and Furuichi clans counterattacked, leading to the disintegration of Tutsui control. Seizing the opportunity, Matsunaga Hisahide, under the Southern Court during the Nanboku-chō period, invaded Yamato and expelled Junnin, subduing the Ochi clan and others, and in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永和|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eiroku era|1=Eiwa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11131293}} (1559) seized the position of provincial protector (Shugo) from Kōfuku-ji. Later, under the Toyotomi regime, Tutsui Junnin reemerged as a warlord in Yamato Province and established a dual system whereby he simultaneously held the status of protector of Yamato Province and acted as the representative of the government-appointed (kanfu) shuto—the temple retainer class—in administering and safeguarding the temple. However, after Junnin's death, Toyotomi Hideyoshi granted Yamato Province to his brother Hidechō, thereby disbanding the existing system, forcing the shuto and the local "kokumin" (the people of the country) to be dissolved; and for Kōfuku-ji, only the original twenty names (as recorded on the official kanfu shuto list) were allowed to serve as temple administrators and security personnel, with a total stipend of 380 koku. == See Also == * {{ill|Yamato Eikyō no Ran|ja|大和永享の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Eikyō no Ran|1=Yamato Eikyō War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433497}} * {{ill|Warrior monk|en|Warrior monk|WD=Q7970798|lt=Warrior Monk}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:衆徒]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:衆徒]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:衆徒]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11625971]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shuto''' (衆徒, しゅと), also known as '''Dōshu''' (堂衆, どうしゅ/どうしゅう), originally refers to the class of Buddhist monks who, from the Heian period onward, resided in large temples and, in addition to engaging in scholarly study and ascetic training, performed the practical administrative duties within the temple. They formed the main body of the temple’s "great multitude" ({{ill|Daisū|ja|大衆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisū|1=general public|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5369389}}), and in some contexts the term "daisū" is used exclusively to refer to these shuto. In particular, at {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|en|Kōfuku-ji|WD=Q1070863|lt=Kōfuku-ji}} in {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province}}, where the temple also served a protective function ({{ill|Shugo|ja|守護職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 守護|1=shugo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1141023}}), the warriors of the country (referred to as ''Yamato Bushi'' (大和武士)) were sometimes called shuto.
== Overview == In general, shuto Rank below the scholarly monks ({{ill|Gakuryo|ja|学侶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gakuryo|1=Gakuryo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448122}}) and above the itinerant monks ({{ill|The Wayfarer (novel)|en|The Wayfarer (novel)|WD=Q5824170|lt=Gyōnin}}). They handled practical tasks within the temple such as security, management, and overall operations. Many shuto came from mid-to-lower class aristocratic families, samurai, or local officials ({{ill|Shōkan|ja|荘官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkan|1=shōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618914}}), and some possessed martial skills; indeed, armed monk soldiers ({{ill|Sōhei|en|Sōhei|WD=Q1475695|lt=Sōhei}}) were largely composed of shuto. (However, at {{ill|Enryaku-ji|en|Enryaku-ji|WD=Q917195|lt=Enryaku-ji}}, the shuto were predominantly drawn from higher-Ranking nobles, and in that temple those expelled from the shuto class organized separately as "dōshu.") They played a central role in temple decision-making and in the enforcement of the temple’s authority (through bodies such as the {{ill|Daisū Shingi|ja|大衆僉議|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisū Shingi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Kyōso|ja|強訴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōso|1=gōso|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487759}}). Later, as monastic hierarchies became more differentiated, scholarly monks ({{ill|Gakuryo|ja|学侶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gakuryo|1=Gakuryo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448122}}) who were of high-Ranking court families ({{ill|Kōkei|ja|Kugyō|1=kugyō|2=ja|3=公卿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōkei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3138036}}) attempted to monopolize key positions and to influence temple management, leading to fierce opposition from the itinerant monks.
== Yamato Warriors == In Yamato Province, where {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|en|Kōfuku-ji|WD=Q1070863|lt=Kōfuku-ji}} had strong connections with the {{ill|Five regent houses|en|Five regent houses|WD=Q1067142|lt=Five regent houses}}, the temple, upon entering the Kamakura period, eventually secured control over Nara and later over the entire Yamato region. Originally, the shuto of this temple were fixed at twenty by the imperial edict (via the {{ill|Taihōkanfu|ja|太政官符|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taihōkanfu|1=Daijō-kan-pu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444013}}), serving as assistants to the bettō and the Sangkō. Over time, however, influential temple families ({{ill|Monzeki|en|Monzeki|WD=Q1079855|lt=Monzeki}}) such as those affiliated with {{ill|Ichijō-in|ja|一乗院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichijō-in|1=Ichijō-in|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11352379}} and {{ill|Daijō-in|ja|大乗院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijō-in|1=Daijō-in|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16766994}} expanded their power by incorporating domestic warriors and local chieftains (often styled as {{ill|Gokenin|en|Gokenin|WD=Q63765|lt=Gokenin}}) into their own retinues, subsequently leading their scholarly monks to assume administrative and security roles within the temple and in the judicial matters ({{ill|Kendan|ja|検断|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kendan|1=Kendan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540935}}) of Nara. Additionally, through the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, the "divine men" ({{ill|Shinjin|ja|神人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinjin|1=jinin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588676}}) of Kasuga Shrine, which had become integrated with Kōfuku-ji, were similarly incorporated, and these Kasuga deities came to be known as "kokumin" (国民).
Notable Yamato shuto include those under the patronage of Ichijō-in—such as members of the {{ill|Tsutsui clan|en|Tsutsui clan|WD=Q776477|lt=Tutsui Clan}} and the {{ill|Ochi Clan|ja|越智氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ochi Clan|1=Ochi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636555}}—and those under Daijō-in—such as members of the {{ill|Toshichi Clan|ja|十市氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshichi Clan|1=Toichi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109600528}} and the {{ill|Furuichi Clan|ja|古市氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furuichi Clan|1=Furuichi Clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109595566}}. In the Nanboku-chō period, Tōshichi Tōyasu invaded Kōfuku-ji’s estates and established his own domain as a local lord. In response to a request from the bettō of Kōfuku-ji for the suppression of Tōshichi Tōyasu, the 3rd shogun of the Muromachi Bakufu, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|en|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|WD=Q248381|lt=Ashikaga Yoshimitsu}}, dispatched commanders such as {{ill|Shiba Yoshiyuki|en|Shiba Yoshiyuki|WD=Q11500863|lt=Shiba Yoshishiro}} and {{ill|Toki Yoriyasu|ja|土岐頼康|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toki Yoriyasu|1=Yoriyasu Toki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423410}} to Yamato in 1379 (Eiwa 5). However, when the Bakufu army showed no signs of commencing an expedition against Tōshichi Tōyasu, Yoshimitsu ordered all the generals of the Tōshichi suppression force to return to Kyoto. Consequently, for the first time in history, the protest by the "Jinboku Nyūroku" (the entry of the sacred tree into the capital) failed, dealing a severe blow to temple power. The 6th shogun, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|en|Ashikaga Yoshinori|WD=Q239170|lt=Ashikaga Yoshinori}}, intervened in the personnel decisions of the influential temple families within Yamato Province in order to establish Bakufu authority. However, due to conflicts between the factions of Tōdaichū-bō and Ido, the situation devolved into a quagmire. In Tōdaichū-bō, factions including Ochi, Hashio, and Manzai allied with Southern Court elements such as Sawa and Akiyama emerged, while in Ido, the Tutsui and Toshichi factions prevailed; the rivalry between the Tutsui and Ochi factions eventually split Yamato into two, sparking the conflict known as the "Yamato Eikyō no Ran." In August of the 3rd year of the {{ill|Civilization|en|Civilization|WD=Q8432|lt=Bunmei era}} (1471), forces led by Fuse (Eastern faction) and Ochi Ie Saka (Western faction) clashed—with Fuse supported by Hashio, Narahara, Kubishira, Toshichi, Tutsui, and Maetaka (Fuse Takada), and Ochi reinforced by Manzai, Hatta, Iitaka, Furuichi, Todata, Koizumi, and Tōtakada (Tōma Takada). With influential guardian daimyō also intervening, the long internal conflict, interspersed with the Ōnin War, eventually ushered in a period of warlord rule during the Sengoku period in Yamato Province.
During the Sengoku period, following the death in battle of {{ill|Hatakeyama clan|en|Hatakeyama clan|WD=Q662881|lt=Hatakeyama clan}} retainer {{ill|Kizawa Nagamasa|en|Kizawa Nagamasa|WD=Q6419175|lt=Kizawa Nagamasa}} in Kawachi Province, the clans led by {{ill|Tsutsui clan|en|Tsutsui clan|WD=Q776477|lt=Tutsui Junshō}} and {{ill|Ochi Clan|ja|越智氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ochi Clan|1=Ochi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636555}} expanded their influence. For a time they controlled much of the country and advanced toward becoming warlords, but after Junshō’s sudden death and the succession by his young heir, {{ill|Tutsui Junnin|ja|十市遠康|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tutsui Junnin|1=Tōchi Tōyasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11405031}}, the Ochi and Furuichi clans counterattacked, leading to the disintegration of Tutsui control. Seizing the opportunity, Matsunaga Hisahide, under the Southern Court during the Nanboku-chō period, invaded Yamato and expelled Junnin, subduing the Ochi clan and others, and in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永和|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eiroku era|1=Eiwa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11131293}} (1559) seized the position of provincial protector (Shugo) from Kōfuku-ji. Later, under the Toyotomi regime, Tutsui Junnin reemerged as a warlord in Yamato Province and established a dual system whereby he simultaneously held the status of protector of Yamato Province and acted as the representative of the government-appointed (kanfu) shuto—the temple retainer class—in administering and safeguarding the temple. However, after Junnin's death, Toyotomi Hideyoshi granted Yamato Province to his brother Hidechō, thereby disbanding the existing system, forcing the shuto and the local "kokumin" (the people of the country) to be dissolved; and for Kōfuku-ji, only the original twenty names (as recorded on the official kanfu shuto list) were allowed to serve as temple administrators and security personnel, with a total stipend of 380 koku. == See Also == * {{ill|Yamato Eikyō no Ran|ja|大和永享の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Eikyō no Ran|1=Yamato Eikyō War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433497}} * {{ill|Warrior monk|en|Warrior monk|WD=Q7970798|lt=Warrior Monk}}

Later, as monastic hierarchies became more differentiated, scholarly monks ({{ill|Gakuryo|ja|学侶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gakuryo|1=Gakuryo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448122}}) who were of high-Ranking court families ({{ill|Kōkei|ja|Kugyō|1=kugyō|2=ja|3=公卿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōkei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3138036}}) attempted to monopolize key positions and to influence temple management, leading to fierce opposition from the itinerant monks.
== Yamato Warriors == In Yamato Province, where {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|en|Kōfuku-ji|WD=Q1070863|lt=Kōfuku-ji}} had strong connections with the {{ill|Five regent houses|en|Five regent houses|WD=Q1067142|lt=Five regent houses}}, the temple, upon entering the Kamakura period, eventually secured control over Nara and later over the entire Yamato region. Originally, the shuto of this temple were fixed at twenty by the imperial edict (via the {{ill|Taihōkanfu|ja|太政官符|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taihōkanfu|1=Daijō-kan-pu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444013}}), serving as assistants to the bettō and the Sangkō. Over time, however, influential temple families ({{ill|Monzeki|en|Monzeki|WD=Q1079855|lt=Monzeki}}) such as those affiliated with {{ill|Ichijō-in|ja|一乗院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichijō-in|1=Ichijō-in|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11352379}} and {{ill|Daijō-in|ja|大乗院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijō-in|1=Daijō-in|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16766994}} expanded their power by incorporating domestic warriors and local chieftains (often styled as {{ill|Gokenin|en|Gokenin|WD=Q63765|lt=Gokenin}}) into their own retinues, subsequently leading their scholarly monks to assume administrative and security roles within the temple and in the judicial matters ({{ill|Kendan|ja|検断|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kendan|1=Kendan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540935}}) of Nara. Additionally, through the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, the "divine men" ({{ill|Shinjin|ja|神人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinjin|1=jinin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588676}}) of Kasuga Shrine, which had become integrated with Kōfuku-ji, were similarly incorporated, and these Kasuga deities came to be known as "kokumin" (国民).
Notable Yamato shuto include those under the patronage of Ichijō-in—such as members of the {{ill|Tsutsui clan|en|Tsutsui clan|WD=Q776477|lt=Tutsui Clan}} and the {{ill|Ochi Clan|ja|越智氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ochi Clan|1=Ochi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636555}}—and those under Daijō-in—such as members of the {{ill|Toshichi Clan|ja|十市氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshichi Clan|1=Toichi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109600528}} and the {{ill|Furuichi Clan|ja|古市氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furuichi Clan|1=Furuichi Clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109595566}}. In the Nanboku-chō period, Tōshichi Tōyasu invaded Kōfuku-ji’s estates and established his own domain as a local lord. In response to a request from the bettō of Kōfuku-ji for the suppression of Tōshichi Tōyasu, the 3rd shogun of the Muromachi Bakufu, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|en|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|WD=Q248381|lt=Ashikaga Yoshimitsu}}, dispatched commanders such as {{ill|Shiba Yoshiyuki|en|Shiba Yoshiyuki|WD=Q11500863|lt=Shiba Yoshishiro}} and {{ill|Toki Yoriyasu|ja|土岐頼康|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toki Yoriyasu|1=Yoriyasu Toki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423410}} to Yamato in 1379 (Eiwa 5). However, when the Bakufu army showed no signs of commencing an expedition against Tōshichi Tōyasu, Yoshimitsu ordered all the generals of the Tōshichi suppression force to return to Kyoto. Consequently, for the first time in history, the protest by the "Jinboku Nyūroku" (the entry of the sacred tree into the capital) failed, dealing a severe blow to temple power. The 6th shogun, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|en|Ashikaga Yoshinori|WD=Q239170|lt=Ashikaga Yoshinori}}, intervened in the personnel decisions of the influential temple families within Yamato Province in order to establish Bakufu authority. However, due to conflicts between the factions of Tōdaichū-bō and Ido, the situation devolved into a quagmire. In Tōdaichū-bō, factions including Ochi, Hashio, and Manzai allied with Southern Court elements such as Sawa and Akiyama emerged, while in Ido, the Tutsui and Toshichi factions prevailed; the rivalry between the Tutsui and Ochi factions eventually split Yamato into two, sparking the conflict known as the "Yamato Eikyō no Ran." In August of the 3rd year of the {{ill|Civilization|en|Civilization|WD=Q8432|lt=Bunmei era}} (1471), forces led by Fuse (Eastern faction) and Ochi Ie Saka (Western faction) clashed—with Fuse supported by Hashio, Narahara, Kubishira, Toshichi, Tutsui, and Maetaka (Fuse Takada), and Ochi reinforced by Manzai, Hatta, Iitaka, Furuichi, Todata, Koizumi, and Tōtakada (Tōma Takada). With influential guardian daimyō also intervening, the long internal conflict, interspersed with the Ōnin War, eventually ushered in a period of warlord rule during the Sengoku period in Yamato Province.
During the Sengoku period, following the death in battle of {{ill|Hatakeyama clan|en|Hatakeyama clan|WD=Q662881|lt=Hatakeyama clan}} retainer {{ill|Kizawa Nagamasa|en|Kizawa Nagamasa|WD=Q6419175|lt=Kizawa Nagamasa}} in Kawachi Province, the clans led by {{ill|Tsutsui clan|en|Tsutsui clan|WD=Q776477|lt=Tutsui Junshō}} and {{ill|Ochi Clan|ja|越智氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ochi Clan|1=Ochi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636555}} expanded their influence. For a time they controlled much of the country and advanced toward becoming warlords, but after Junshō’s sudden death and the succession by his young heir, {{ill|Tutsui Junnin|ja|十市遠康|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tutsui Junnin|1=Tōchi Tōyasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11405031}}, the Ochi and Furuichi clans counterattacked, leading to the disintegration of Tutsui control. Seizing the opportunity, Matsunaga Hisahide, under the Southern Court during the Nanboku-chō period, invaded Yamato and expelled Junnin, subduing the Ochi clan and others, and in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永和|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eiroku era|1=Eiwa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11131293}} (1559) seized the position of provincial protector (Shugo) from Kōfuku-ji. Later, under the Toyotomi regime, Tutsui Junnin reemerged as a warlord in Yamato Province and established a dual system whereby he simultaneously held the status of protector of Yamato Province and acted as the representative of the government-appointed (kanfu) shuto—the temple retainer class—in administering and safeguarding the temple. However, after Junnin's death, Toyotomi Hideyoshi granted Yamato Province to his brother Hidechō, thereby disbanding the existing system, forcing the shuto and the local "kokumin" (the people of the country) to be dissolved; and for Kōfuku-ji, only the original twenty names (as recorded on the official kanfu shuto list) were allowed to serve as temple administrators and security personnel, with a total stipend of 380 koku. == See Also == * {{ill|Yamato Eikyō no Ran|ja|大和永享の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Eikyō no Ran|1=Yamato Eikyō War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433497}} * {{ill|Warrior monk|en|Warrior monk|WD=Q7970798|lt=Warrior Monk}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:衆徒]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:衆徒]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:衆徒]]
'''Shuto''' (衆徒, しゅと), also known as '''Dōshu''' (堂衆, どうしゅ/どうしゅう), originally refers to the class of Buddhist monks who, from the Heian period onward, resided in large temples and, in addition to engaging in scholarly study and ascetic training, performed the practical administrative duties within the temple. They formed the main body of the temple’s "great multitude" ({{ill|Daisū|ja|大衆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisū|1=general public|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5369389}}), and in some contexts the term "daisū" is used exclusively to refer to these shuto. In particular, at {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|en|Kōfuku-ji|WD=Q1070863|lt=Kōfuku-ji}} in {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province}}, where the temple also served a protective function ({{ill|Shugo|ja|守護職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 守護|1=shugo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1141023}}), the warriors of the country (referred to as ''Yamato Bushi'' (大和武士)) were sometimes called shuto.
== Overview == In general, shuto Rank below the scholarly monks ({{ill|Gakuryo|ja|学侶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gakuryo|1=Gakuryo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448122}}) and above the itinerant monks ({{ill|The Wayfarer (novel)|en|The Wayfarer (novel)|WD=Q5824170|lt=Gyōnin}}). They handled practical tasks within the temple such as security, management, and overall operations. Many shuto came from mid-to-lower class aristocratic families, samurai, or local officials ({{ill|Shōkan|ja|荘官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkan|1=shōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618914}}), and some possessed martial skills; indeed, armed monk soldiers ({{ill|Sōhei|en|Sōhei|WD=Q1475695|lt=Sōhei}}) were largely composed of shuto. (However, at {{ill|Enryaku-ji|en|Enryaku-ji|WD=Q917195|lt=Enryaku-ji}}, the shuto were predominantly drawn from higher-Ranking nobles, and in that temple those expelled from the shuto class organized separately as "dōshu.") They played a central role in temple decision-making and in the enforcement of the temple’s authority (through bodies such as the {{ill|Daisū Shingi|ja|大衆僉議|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisū Shingi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Kyōso|ja|強訴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōso|1=gōso|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487759}}). Later, as monastic hierarchies became more differentiated, scholarly monks ({{ill|Gakuryo|ja|学侶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gakuryo|1=Gakuryo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448122}}) who were of high-Ranking court families ({{ill|Kōkei|ja|Kugyō|1=kugyō|2=ja|3=公卿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōkei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3138036}}) attempted to monopolize key positions and to influence temple management, leading to fierce opposition from the itinerant monks.
== Yamato Warriors == In Yamato Province, where {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|en|Kōfuku-ji|WD=Q1070863|lt=Kōfuku-ji}} had strong connections with the {{ill|Five regent houses|en|Five regent houses|WD=Q1067142|lt=Five regent houses}}, the temple, upon entering the Kamakura period, eventually secured control over Nara and later over the entire Yamato region. Originally, the shuto of this temple were fixed at twenty by the imperial edict (via the {{ill|Taihōkanfu|ja|太政官符|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taihōkanfu|1=Daijō-kan-pu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444013}}), serving as assistants to the bettō and the Sangkō. Over time, however, influential temple families ({{ill|Monzeki|en|Monzeki|WD=Q1079855|lt=Monzeki}}) such as those affiliated with {{ill|Ichijō-in|ja|一乗院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichijō-in|1=Ichijō-in|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11352379}} and {{ill|Daijō-in|ja|大乗院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijō-in|1=Daijō-in|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16766994}} expanded their power by incorporating domestic warriors and local chieftains (often styled as {{ill|Gokenin|en|Gokenin|WD=Q63765|lt=Gokenin}}) into their own retinues, subsequently leading their scholarly monks to assume administrative and security roles within the temple and in the judicial matters ({{ill|Kendan|ja|検断|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kendan|1=Kendan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540935}}) of Nara. Additionally, through the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, the "divine men" ({{ill|Shinjin|ja|神人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinjin|1=jinin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588676}}) of Kasuga Shrine, which had become integrated with Kōfuku-ji, were similarly incorporated, and these Kasuga deities came to be known as "kokumin" (国民).
Notable Yamato shuto include those under the patronage of Ichijō-in—such as members of the {{ill|Tsutsui clan|en|Tsutsui clan|WD=Q776477|lt=Tutsui Clan}} and the {{ill|Ochi Clan|ja|越智氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ochi Clan|1=Ochi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636555}}—and those under Daijō-in—such as members of the {{ill|Toshichi Clan|ja|十市氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshichi Clan|1=Toichi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109600528}} and the {{ill|Furuichi Clan|ja|古市氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furuichi Clan|1=Furuichi Clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109595566}}. In the Nanboku-chō period, Tōshichi Tōyasu invaded Kōfuku-ji’s estates and established his own domain as a local lord. In response to a request from the bettō of Kōfuku-ji for the suppression of Tōshichi Tōyasu, the 3rd shogun of the Muromachi Bakufu, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|en|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|WD=Q248381|lt=Ashikaga Yoshimitsu}}, dispatched commanders such as {{ill|Shiba Yoshiyuki|en|Shiba Yoshiyuki|WD=Q11500863|lt=Shiba Yoshishiro}} and {{ill|Toki Yoriyasu|ja|土岐頼康|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toki Yoriyasu|1=Yoriyasu Toki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423410}} to Yamato in 1379 (Eiwa 5). However, when the Bakufu army showed no signs of commencing an expedition against Tōshichi Tōyasu, Yoshimitsu ordered all the generals of the Tōshichi suppression force to return to Kyoto. Consequently, for the first time in history, the protest by the "Jinboku Nyūroku" (the entry of the sacred tree into the capital) failed, dealing a severe blow to temple power. The 6th shogun, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|en|Ashikaga Yoshinori|WD=Q239170|lt=Ashikaga Yoshinori}}, intervened in the personnel decisions of the influential temple families within Yamato Province in order to establish Bakufu authority. However, due to conflicts between the factions of Tōdaichū-bō and Ido, the situation devolved into a quagmire. In Tōdaichū-bō, factions including Ochi, Hashio, and Manzai allied with Southern Court elements such as Sawa and Akiyama emerged, while in Ido, the Tutsui and Toshichi factions prevailed; the rivalry between the Tutsui and Ochi factions eventually split Yamato into two, sparking the conflict known as the "Yamato Eikyō no Ran." In August of the 3rd year of the {{ill|Civilization|en|Civilization|WD=Q8432|lt=Bunmei era}} (1471), forces led by Fuse (Eastern faction) and Ochi Ie Saka (Western faction) clashed—with Fuse supported by Hashio, Narahara, Kubishira, Toshichi, Tutsui, and Maetaka (Fuse Takada), and Ochi reinforced by Manzai, Hatta, Iitaka, Furuichi, Todata, Koizumi, and Tōtakada (Tōma Takada). With influential guardian daimyō also intervening, the long internal conflict, interspersed with the Ōnin War, eventually ushered in a period of warlord rule during the Sengoku period in Yamato Province.
During the Sengoku period, following the death in battle of {{ill|Hatakeyama clan|en|Hatakeyama clan|WD=Q662881|lt=Hatakeyama clan}} retainer {{ill|Kizawa Nagamasa|en|Kizawa Nagamasa|WD=Q6419175|lt=Kizawa Nagamasa}} in Kawachi Province, the clans led by {{ill|Tsutsui clan|en|Tsutsui clan|WD=Q776477|lt=Tutsui Junshō}} and {{ill|Ochi Clan|ja|越智氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ochi Clan|1=Ochi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636555}} expanded their influence. For a time they controlled much of the country and advanced toward becoming warlords, but after Junshō’s sudden death and the succession by his young heir, {{ill|Tutsui Junnin|ja|十市遠康|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tutsui Junnin|1=Tōchi Tōyasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11405031}}, the Ochi and Furuichi clans counterattacked, leading to the disintegration of Tutsui control. Seizing the opportunity, Matsunaga Hisahide, under the Southern Court during the Nanboku-chō period, invaded Yamato and expelled Junnin, subduing the Ochi clan and others, and in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永和|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eiroku era|1=Eiwa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11131293}} (1559) seized the position of provincial protector (Shugo) from Kōfuku-ji. Later, under the Toyotomi regime, Tutsui Junnin reemerged as a warlord in Yamato Province and established a dual system whereby he simultaneously held the status of protector of Yamato Province and acted as the representative of the government-appointed (kanfu) shuto—the temple retainer class—in administering and safeguarding the temple. However, after Junnin's death, Toyotomi Hideyoshi granted Yamato Province to his brother Hidechō, thereby disbanding the existing system, forcing the shuto and the local "kokumin" (the people of the country) to be dissolved; and for Kōfuku-ji, only the original twenty names (as recorded on the official kanfu shuto list) were allowed to serve as temple administrators and security personnel, with a total stipend of 380 koku. == See Also == * {{ill|Yamato Eikyō no Ran|ja|大和永享の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Eikyō no Ran|1=Yamato Eikyō War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433497}} * {{ill|Warrior monk|en|Warrior monk|WD=Q7970798|lt=Warrior Monk}}

They formed the main body of the temple’s "great multitude" ({{ill|Daisū|ja|大衆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisū|1=general public|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5369389}}), and in some contexts the term "daisū" is used exclusively to refer to these shuto. In particular, at {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|en|Kōfuku-ji|WD=Q1070863|lt=Kōfuku-ji}} in {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province}}, where the temple also served a protective function ({{ill|Shugo|ja|守護職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 守護|1=shugo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1141023}}), the warriors of the country (referred to as ''Yamato Bushi'' (大和武士)) were sometimes called shuto.
== Overview == In general, shuto Rank below the scholarly monks ({{ill|Gakuryo|ja|学侶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gakuryo|1=Gakuryo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448122}}) and above the itinerant monks ({{ill|The Wayfarer (novel)|en|The Wayfarer (novel)|WD=Q5824170|lt=Gyōnin}}). They handled practical tasks within the temple such as security, management, and overall operations. Many shuto came from mid-to-lower class aristocratic families, samurai, or local officials ({{ill|Shōkan|ja|荘官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkan|1=shōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618914}}), and some possessed martial skills; indeed, armed monk soldiers ({{ill|Sōhei|en|Sōhei|WD=Q1475695|lt=Sōhei}}) were largely composed of shuto. (However, at {{ill|Enryaku-ji|en|Enryaku-ji|WD=Q917195|lt=Enryaku-ji}}, the shuto were predominantly drawn from higher-Ranking nobles, and in that temple those expelled from the shuto class organized separately as "dōshu.") They played a central role in temple decision-making and in the enforcement of the temple’s authority (through bodies such as the {{ill|Daisū Shingi|ja|大衆僉議|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisū Shingi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Kyōso|ja|強訴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōso|1=gōso|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487759}}). Later, as monastic hierarchies became more differentiated, scholarly monks ({{ill|Gakuryo|ja|学侶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gakuryo|1=Gakuryo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448122}}) who were of high-Ranking court families ({{ill|Kōkei|ja|Kugyō|1=kugyō|2=ja|3=公卿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōkei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3138036}}) attempted to monopolize key positions and to influence temple management, leading to fierce opposition from the itinerant monks.
== Yamato Warriors == In Yamato Province, where {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|en|Kōfuku-ji|WD=Q1070863|lt=Kōfuku-ji}} had strong connections with the {{ill|Five regent houses|en|Five regent houses|WD=Q1067142|lt=Five regent houses}}, the temple, upon entering the Kamakura period, eventually secured control over Nara and later over the entire Yamato region. Originally, the shuto of this temple were fixed at twenty by the imperial edict (via the {{ill|Taihōkanfu|ja|太政官符|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taihōkanfu|1=Daijō-kan-pu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444013}}), serving as assistants to the bettō and the Sangkō. Over time, however, influential temple families ({{ill|Monzeki|en|Monzeki|WD=Q1079855|lt=Monzeki}}) such as those affiliated with {{ill|Ichijō-in|ja|一乗院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichijō-in|1=Ichijō-in|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11352379}} and {{ill|Daijō-in|ja|大乗院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijō-in|1=Daijō-in|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16766994}} expanded their power by incorporating domestic warriors and local chieftains (often styled as {{ill|Gokenin|en|Gokenin|WD=Q63765|lt=Gokenin}}) into their own retinues, subsequently leading their scholarly monks to assume administrative and security roles within the temple and in the judicial matters ({{ill|Kendan|ja|検断|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kendan|1=Kendan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540935}}) of Nara. Additionally, through the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, the "divine men" ({{ill|Shinjin|ja|神人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinjin|1=jinin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588676}}) of Kasuga Shrine, which had become integrated with Kōfuku-ji, were similarly incorporated, and these Kasuga deities came to be known as "kokumin" (国民).
Notable Yamato shuto include those under the patronage of Ichijō-in—such as members of the {{ill|Tsutsui clan|en|Tsutsui clan|WD=Q776477|lt=Tutsui Clan}} and the {{ill|Ochi Clan|ja|越智氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ochi Clan|1=Ochi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636555}}—and those under Daijō-in—such as members of the {{ill|Toshichi Clan|ja|十市氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshichi Clan|1=Toichi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109600528}} and the {{ill|Furuichi Clan|ja|古市氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furuichi Clan|1=Furuichi Clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109595566}}. In the Nanboku-chō period, Tōshichi Tōyasu invaded Kōfuku-ji’s estates and established his own domain as a local lord. In response to a request from the bettō of Kōfuku-ji for the suppression of Tōshichi Tōyasu, the 3rd shogun of the Muromachi Bakufu, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|en|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|WD=Q248381|lt=Ashikaga Yoshimitsu}}, dispatched commanders such as {{ill|Shiba Yoshiyuki|en|Shiba Yoshiyuki|WD=Q11500863|lt=Shiba Yoshishiro}} and {{ill|Toki Yoriyasu|ja|土岐頼康|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toki Yoriyasu|1=Yoriyasu Toki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423410}} to Yamato in 1379 (Eiwa 5). However, when the Bakufu army showed no signs of commencing an expedition against Tōshichi Tōyasu, Yoshimitsu ordered all the generals of the Tōshichi suppression force to return to Kyoto. Consequently, for the first time in history, the protest by the "Jinboku Nyūroku" (the entry of the sacred tree into the capital) failed, dealing a severe blow to temple power. The 6th shogun, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|en|Ashikaga Yoshinori|WD=Q239170|lt=Ashikaga Yoshinori}}, intervened in the personnel decisions of the influential temple families within Yamato Province in order to establish Bakufu authority. However, due to conflicts between the factions of Tōdaichū-bō and Ido, the situation devolved into a quagmire. In Tōdaichū-bō, factions including Ochi, Hashio, and Manzai allied with Southern Court elements such as Sawa and Akiyama emerged, while in Ido, the Tutsui and Toshichi factions prevailed; the rivalry between the Tutsui and Ochi factions eventually split Yamato into two, sparking the conflict known as the "Yamato Eikyō no Ran." In August of the 3rd year of the {{ill|Civilization|en|Civilization|WD=Q8432|lt=Bunmei era}} (1471), forces led by Fuse (Eastern faction) and Ochi Ie Saka (Western faction) clashed—with Fuse supported by Hashio, Narahara, Kubishira, Toshichi, Tutsui, and Maetaka (Fuse Takada), and Ochi reinforced by Manzai, Hatta, Iitaka, Furuichi, Todata, Koizumi, and Tōtakada (Tōma Takada). With influential guardian daimyō also intervening, the long internal conflict, interspersed with the Ōnin War, eventually ushered in a period of warlord rule during the Sengoku period in Yamato Province.
During the Sengoku period, following the death in battle of {{ill|Hatakeyama clan|en|Hatakeyama clan|WD=Q662881|lt=Hatakeyama clan}} retainer {{ill|Kizawa Nagamasa|en|Kizawa Nagamasa|WD=Q6419175|lt=Kizawa Nagamasa}} in Kawachi Province, the clans led by {{ill|Tsutsui clan|en|Tsutsui clan|WD=Q776477|lt=Tutsui Junshō}} and {{ill|Ochi Clan|ja|越智氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ochi Clan|1=Ochi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636555}} expanded their influence. For a time they controlled much of the country and advanced toward becoming warlords, but after Junshō’s sudden death and the succession by his young heir, {{ill|Tutsui Junnin|ja|十市遠康|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tutsui Junnin|1=Tōchi Tōyasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11405031}}, the Ochi and Furuichi clans counterattacked, leading to the disintegration of Tutsui control. Seizing the opportunity, Matsunaga Hisahide, under the Southern Court during the Nanboku-chō period, invaded Yamato and expelled Junnin, subduing the Ochi clan and others, and in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永和|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eiroku era|1=Eiwa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11131293}} (1559) seized the position of provincial protector (Shugo) from Kōfuku-ji. Later, under the Toyotomi regime, Tutsui Junnin reemerged as a warlord in Yamato Province and established a dual system whereby he simultaneously held the status of protector of Yamato Province and acted as the representative of the government-appointed (kanfu) shuto—the temple retainer class—in administering and safeguarding the temple. However, after Junnin's death, Toyotomi Hideyoshi granted Yamato Province to his brother Hidechō, thereby disbanding the existing system, forcing the shuto and the local "kokumin" (the people of the country) to be dissolved; and for Kōfuku-ji, only the original twenty names (as recorded on the official kanfu shuto list) were allowed to serve as temple administrators and security personnel, with a total stipend of 380 koku. == See Also == * {{ill|Yamato Eikyō no Ran|ja|大和永享の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Eikyō no Ran|1=Yamato Eikyō War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433497}} * {{ill|Warrior monk|en|Warrior monk|WD=Q7970798|lt=Warrior Monk}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:衆徒]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:衆徒]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:衆徒]]
'''Shuto''' (衆徒, しゅと), also known as '''Dōshu''' (堂衆, どうしゅ/どうしゅう), originally refers to the class of Buddhist monks who, from the Heian period onward, resided in large temples and, in addition to engaging in scholarly study and ascetic training, performed the practical administrative duties within the temple. They formed the main body of the temple’s "great multitude" ({{ill|Daisū|ja|大衆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisū|1=general public|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5369389}}), and in some contexts the term "daisū" is used exclusively to refer to these shuto. In particular, at {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|en|Kōfuku-ji|WD=Q1070863|lt=Kōfuku-ji|1=Kōfuku-ji}} in {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province|1=Yamato Province}}, where the temple also served a protective function ({{ill|Shugo|ja|守護職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 守護|1=Shugo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1141023}}), the warriors of the country (referred to as ''Yamato Bushi'' (大和武士)) were sometimes called shuto.
== Overview == In general, shuto Rank below the scholarly monks ({{ill|Gakuryo|ja|学侶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gakuryo|1=Q11448122|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448122}}) and above the itinerant monks ({{ill|The Wayfarer (novel)|en|The Wayfarer (novel)|WD=Q5824170|lt=Gyōnin|1=The Wayfarer (novel)}}). They handled practical tasks within the temple such as security, management, and overall operations. Many shuto came from mid-to-lower class aristocratic families, samurai, or local officials ({{ill|Shōkan|ja|荘官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkan|1=shōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618914}}), and some possessed martial skills; indeed, armed monk soldiers ({{ill|Sōhei|en|Sōhei|WD=Q1475695|lt=Sōhei|1=Sōhei}}) were largely composed of shuto. (However, at {{ill|Enryaku-ji|en|Enryaku-ji|WD=Q917195|lt=Enryaku-ji|1=Enryaku-ji}}, the shuto were predominantly drawn from higher-Ranking nobles, and in that temple those expelled from the shuto class organized separately as "dōshu.") They played a central role in temple decision-making and in the enforcement of the temple’s authority (through bodies such as the {{ill|Daisū Shingi|ja|大衆僉議|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisū Shingi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Kyōso|ja|強訴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōso|1=Goso|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487759}}). Later, as monastic hierarchies became more differentiated, scholarly monks ({{ill|Gakuryo|ja|学侶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gakuryo|1=Q11448122|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448122}}) who were of high-Ranking court families ({{ill|Kōkei|ja|Kugyō|1=Kugyō|2=ja|3=公卿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōkei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3138036}}) attempted to monopolize key positions and to influence temple management, leading to fierce opposition from the itinerant monks.
== Yamato Warriors == In Yamato Province, where {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|en|Kōfuku-ji|WD=Q1070863|lt=Kōfuku-ji|1=Kōfuku-ji}} had strong connections with the {{ill|Five regent houses|en|Five regent houses|WD=Q1067142|lt=Five regent houses|1=Five regent houses}}, the temple, upon entering the Kamakura period, eventually secured control over Nara and later over the entire Yamato region. Originally, the shuto of this temple were fixed at twenty by the imperial edict (via the {{ill|Taihōkanfu|ja|太政官符|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taihōkanfu|1=Daijō-kan-pu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444013}}), serving as assistants to the bettō and the Sangkō. Over time, however, influential temple families ({{ill|Monzeki|en|Monzeki|WD=Q1079855|lt=Monzeki|1=Monzeki}}) such as those affiliated with {{ill|Ichijō-in|ja|一乗院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichijō-in|1=Ichijō-in|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11352379}} and {{ill|Daijō-in|ja|大乗院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijō-in|1=Daijō-in|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16766994}} expanded their power by incorporating domestic warriors and local chieftains (often styled as {{ill|Gokenin|en|Gokenin|WD=Q63765|lt=Gokenin|1=Gokenin}}) into their own retinues, subsequently leading their scholarly monks to assume administrative and security roles within the temple and in the judicial matters ({{ill|Kendan|ja|検断|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kendan|1=Q11540935|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540935}}) of Nara. Additionally, through the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, the "divine men" ({{ill|Shinjin|ja|神人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinjin|1=Jinin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588676}}) of Kasuga Shrine, which had become integrated with Kōfuku-ji, were similarly incorporated, and these Kasuga deities came to be known as "kokumin" (国民).
Notable Yamato shuto include those under the patronage of Ichijō-in—such as members of the {{ill|Tsutsui clan|en|Tsutsui clan|WD=Q776477|lt=Tutsui Clan|1=Tsutsui clan}} and the {{ill|Ochi Clan|ja|越智氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ochi Clan|1=Ochi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636555}}—and those under Daijō-in—such as members of the {{ill|Toshichi Clan|ja|十市氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshichi Clan|1=Toichi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109600528}} and the {{ill|Furuichi Clan|ja|古市氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furuichi Clan|1=Q109595566|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109595566}}. In response to a request from the bettō of Kōfuku-ji for the suppression of Tōshichi Tōyasu, the 3rd shogun of the Muromachi Bakufu, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|en|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|WD=Q248381|lt=Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|1=Ashikaga Yoshimitsu}}, dispatched commanders such as {{ill|Shiba Yoshiyuki|en|Shiba Yoshiyuki|WD=Q11500863|lt=Shiba Yoshishiro|1=Shiba Yoshiyuki}} and {{ill|Toki Yoriyasu|ja|土岐頼康|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toki Yoriyasu|1=Yoriyasu Toki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423410}} to Yamato in 1379 (Eiwa 5). However, when the Bakufu army showed no signs of commencing an expedition against Tōshichi Tōyasu, Yoshimitsu ordered all the generals of the Tōshichi suppression force to return to Kyoto. Consequently, for the first time in history, the protest by the "Jinboku Nyūroku" (the entry of the sacred tree into the capital) failed, dealing a severe blow to temple power. The 6th shogun, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|en|Ashikaga Yoshinori|WD=Q239170|lt=Ashikaga Yoshinori|1=Ashikaga Yoshinori}}, intervened in the personnel decisions of the influential temple families within Yamato Province in order to establish Bakufu authority. However, due to conflicts between the factions of Tōdaichū-bō and Ido, the situation devolved into a quagmire. In Tōdaichū-bō, factions including Ochi, Hashio, and Manzai allied with Southern Court elements such as Sawa and Akiyama emerged, while in Ido, the Tutsui and Toshichi factions prevailed; the rivalry between the Tutsui and Ochi factions eventually split Yamato into two, sparking the conflict known as the "Yamato Eikyō no Ran." In August of the 3rd year of the {{ill|Civilization|en|Civilization|WD=Q8432|lt=Bunmei era|1=Civilization}} (1471), forces led by Fuse (Eastern faction) and Ochi Ie Saka (Western faction) clashed—with Fuse supported by Hashio, Narahara, Kubishira, Toshichi, Tutsui, and Maetaka (Fuse Takada), and Ochi reinforced by Manzai, Hatta, Iitaka, Furuichi, Todata, Koizumi, and Tōtakada (Tōma Takada). With influential guardian daimyō also intervening, the long internal conflict, interspersed with the Ōnin War, eventually ushered in a period of warlord rule during the Sengoku period in Yamato Province.
During the Sengoku period, following the death in battle of {{ill|Hatakeyama clan|en|Hatakeyama clan|WD=Q662881|lt=Hatakeyama clan|1=Hatakeyama clan}} retainer {{ill|Kizawa Nagamasa|en|Kizawa Nagamasa|WD=Q6419175|lt=Kizawa Nagamasa|1=Kizawa Nagamasa}} in Kawachi Province, the clans led by {{ill|Tsutsui clan|en|Tsutsui clan|WD=Q776477|lt=Tutsui Junshō|1=Tsutsui clan}} and {{ill|Ochi Clan|ja|越智氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ochi Clan|1=Ochi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636555}} expanded their influence. For a time they controlled much of the country and advanced toward becoming warlords, but after Junshō’s sudden death and the succession by his young heir, {{ill|Tutsui Junnin|ja|十市遠康|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tutsui Junnin|1=Tōchi Tōyasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11405031}}, the Ochi and Furuichi clans counterattacked, leading to the disintegration of Tutsui control. Seizing the opportunity, Matsunaga Hisahide, under the Southern Court during the Nanboku-chō period, invaded Yamato and expelled Junnin, subduing the Ochi clan and others, and in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永和|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eiroku era|1=Eiwa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11131293}} (1559) seized the position of provincial protector (Shugo) from Kōfuku-ji. However, after Junnin's death, Toyotomi Hideyoshi granted Yamato Province to his brother Hidechō, thereby disbanding the existing system, forcing the shuto and the local "kokumin" (the people of the country) to be dissolved; and for Kōfuku-ji, only the original twenty names (as recorded on the official kanfu shuto list) were allowed to serve as temple administrators and security personnel, with a total stipend of 380 koku. == See Also == * {{ill|Yamato Eikyō no Ran|ja|大和永享の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Eikyō no Ran|1=Yamato Eikyō War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433497}} * {{ill|Warrior monk|en|Warrior monk|WD=Q7970798|lt=Warrior Monk|1=Warrior monk}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:衆徒]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:衆徒]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:衆徒]]
'''Shuto''' (衆徒, しゅと), also known as '''Dōshu''' (堂衆, どうしゅ/どうしゅう), originally refers to the class of Buddhist monks who, from the Heian period onward, resided in large temples and, in addition to engaging in scholarly study and ascetic training, performed the practical administrative duties within the temple. They formed the main body of the temple’s "great multitude" ({{ill|Daisū|ja|大衆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisū|1=general public|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5369389}}), and in some contexts the term "daisū" is used exclusively to refer to these shuto. In particular, at {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|en|Kōfuku-ji|WD=Q1070863|lt=Kōfuku-ji|1=Kōfuku-ji}} in {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province|1=Yamato Province}}, where the temple also served a protective function ({{ill|Shugo|ja|守護職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 守護|1=Shugo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1141023}}), the warriors of the country (referred to as ''Yamato Bushi'' (大和武士)) were sometimes called shuto.
== Overview == In general, shuto Rank below the scholarly monks ({{ill|Gakuryo|ja|学侶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gakuryo|1=Q11448122|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448122}}) and above the itinerant monks ({{ill|The Wayfarer (novel)|en|The Wayfarer (novel)|WD=Q5824170|lt=Gyōnin|1=The Wayfarer (novel)}}). They handled practical tasks within the temple such as security, management, and overall operations. Many shuto came from mid-to-lower class aristocratic families, samurai, or local officials ({{ill|Shōkan|ja|荘官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkan|1=shōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618914}}), and some possessed martial skills; indeed, armed monk soldiers ({{ill|Sōhei|en|Sōhei|WD=Q1475695|lt=Sōhei|1=Sōhei}}) were largely composed of shuto. (However, at {{ill|Enryaku-ji|en|Enryaku-ji|WD=Q917195|lt=Enryaku-ji|1=Enryaku-ji}}, the shuto were predominantly drawn from higher-Ranking nobles, and in that temple those expelled from the shuto class organized separately as "dōshu.") They played a central role in temple decision-making and in the enforcement of the temple’s authority (through bodies such as the {{ill|Daisū Shingi|ja|大衆僉議|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisū Shingi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Kyōso|ja|強訴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōso|1=Goso|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487759}}). Later, as monastic hierarchies became more differentiated, scholarly monks ({{ill|Gakuryo|ja|学侶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gakuryo|1=Q11448122|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448122}}) who were of high-Ranking court families ({{ill|Kōkei|ja|Kugyō|1=Kugyō|2=ja|3=公卿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōkei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3138036}}) attempted to monopolize key positions and to influence temple management, leading to fierce opposition from the itinerant monks.
== Yamato Warriors == In Yamato Province, where {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|en|Kōfuku-ji|WD=Q1070863|lt=Kōfuku-ji|1=Kōfuku-ji}} had strong connections with the {{ill|Five regent houses|en|Five regent houses|WD=Q1067142|lt=Five regent houses|1=Five regent houses}}, the temple, upon entering the Kamakura period, eventually secured control over Nara and later over the entire Yamato region. Originally, the shuto of this temple were fixed at twenty by the imperial edict (via the {{ill|Taihōkanfu|ja|太政官符|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taihōkanfu|1=Daijō-kan-pu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11444013}}), serving as assistants to the bettō and the Sangkō. Over time, however, influential temple families ({{ill|Monzeki|en|Monzeki|WD=Q1079855|lt=Monzeki|1=Monzeki}}) such as those affiliated with {{ill|Ichijō-in|ja|一乗院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichijō-in|1=Ichijō-in|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11352379}} and {{ill|Daijō-in|ja|大乗院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijō-in|1=Daijō-in|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16766994}} expanded their power by incorporating domestic warriors and local chieftains (often styled as {{ill|Gokenin|en|Gokenin|WD=Q63765|lt=Gokenin|1=Gokenin}}) into their own retinues, subsequently leading their scholarly monks to assume administrative and security roles within the temple and in the judicial matters ({{ill|Kendan|ja|検断|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kendan|1=Q11540935|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540935}}) of Nara. Additionally, through the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, the "divine men" ({{ill|Shinjin|ja|神人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinjin|1=Jinin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588676}}) of Kasuga Shrine, which had become integrated with Kōfuku-ji, were similarly incorporated, and these Kasuga deities came to be known as "kokumin" (国民).
Notable Yamato shuto include those under the patronage of Ichijō-in—such as members of the {{ill|Tsutsui clan|en|Tsutsui clan|WD=Q776477|lt=Tutsui Clan|1=Tsutsui clan}} and the {{ill|Ochi Clan|ja|越智氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ochi Clan|1=Ochi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636555}}—and those under Daijō-in—such as members of the {{ill|Toshichi Clan|ja|十市氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshichi Clan|1=Toichi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109600528}} and the {{ill|Furuichi Clan|ja|古市氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furuichi Clan|1=Q109595566|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109595566}}. In the Nanboku-chō period, Tōshichi Tōyasu invaded Kōfuku-ji’s estates and established his own domain as a local lord. In response to a request from the bettō of Kōfuku-ji for the suppression of Tōshichi Tōyasu, the 3rd shogun of the Muromachi Bakufu, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|en|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|WD=Q248381|lt=Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|1=Ashikaga Yoshimitsu}}, dispatched commanders such as {{ill|Shiba Yoshiyuki|en|Shiba Yoshiyuki|WD=Q11500863|lt=Shiba Yoshishiro|1=Shiba Yoshiyuki}} and {{ill|Toki Yoriyasu|ja|土岐頼康|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toki Yoriyasu|1=Yoriyasu Toki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423410}} to Yamato in 1379 (Eiwa 5). However, when the Bakufu army showed no signs of commencing an expedition against Tōshichi Tōyasu, Yoshimitsu ordered all the generals of the Tōshichi suppression force to return to Kyoto. Consequently, for the first time in history, the protest by the "Jinboku Nyūroku" (the entry of the sacred tree into the capital) failed, dealing a severe blow to temple power. The 6th shogun, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|en|Ashikaga Yoshinori|WD=Q239170|lt=Ashikaga Yoshinori|1=Ashikaga Yoshinori}}, intervened in the personnel decisions of the influential temple families within Yamato Province in order to establish Bakufu authority. However, due to conflicts between the factions of Tōdaichū-bō and Ido, the situation devolved into a quagmire. In Tōdaichū-bō, factions including Ochi, Hashio, and Manzai allied with Southern Court elements such as Sawa and Akiyama emerged, while in Ido, the Tutsui and Toshichi factions prevailed; the rivalry between the Tutsui and Ochi factions eventually split Yamato into two, sparking the conflict known as the "Yamato Eikyō no Ran." In August of the 3rd year of the {{ill|Civilization|en|Civilization|WD=Q8432|lt=Bunmei era|1=Civilization}} (1471), forces led by Fuse (Eastern faction) and Ochi Ie Saka (Western faction) clashed—with Fuse supported by Hashio, Narahara, Kubishira, Toshichi, Tutsui, and Maetaka (Fuse Takada), and Ochi reinforced by Manzai, Hatta, Iitaka, Furuichi, Todata, Koizumi, and Tōtakada (Tōma Takada). With influential guardian daimyō also intervening, the long internal conflict, interspersed with the Ōnin War, eventually ushered in a period of warlord rule during the Sengoku period in Yamato Province.
During the Sengoku period, following the death in battle of {{ill|Hatakeyama clan|en|Hatakeyama clan|WD=Q662881|lt=Hatakeyama clan|1=Hatakeyama clan}} retainer {{ill|Kizawa Nagamasa|en|Kizawa Nagamasa|WD=Q6419175|lt=Kizawa Nagamasa|1=Kizawa Nagamasa}} in Kawachi Province, the clans led by {{ill|Tsutsui clan|en|Tsutsui clan|WD=Q776477|lt=Tutsui Junshō|1=Tsutsui clan}} and {{ill|Ochi Clan|ja|越智氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ochi Clan|1=Ochi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636555}} expanded their influence. For a time they controlled much of the country and advanced toward becoming warlords, but after Junshō’s sudden death and the succession by his young heir, {{ill|Tutsui Junnin|ja|十市遠康|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tutsui Junnin|1=Tōchi Tōyasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11405031}}, the Ochi and Furuichi clans counterattacked, leading to the disintegration of Tutsui control. Seizing the opportunity, Matsunaga Hisahide, under the Southern Court during the Nanboku-chō period, invaded Yamato and expelled Junnin, subduing the Ochi clan and others, and in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永和|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eiroku era|1=Eiwa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11131293}} (1559) seized the position of provincial protector (Shugo) from Kōfuku-ji. Later, under the Toyotomi regime, Tutsui Junnin reemerged as a warlord in Yamato Province and established a dual system whereby he simultaneously held the status of protector of Yamato Province and acted as the representative of the government-appointed (kanfu) shuto—the temple retainer class—in administering and safeguarding the temple. However, after Junnin's death, Toyotomi Hideyoshi granted Yamato Province to his brother Hidechō, thereby disbanding the existing system, forcing the shuto and the local "kokumin" (the people of the country) to be dissolved; and for Kōfuku-ji, only the original twenty names (as recorded on the official kanfu shuto list) were allowed to serve as temple administrators and security personnel, with a total stipend of 380 koku. == See Also == * {{ill|Yamato Eikyō no Ran|ja|大和永享の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Eikyō no Ran|1=Yamato Eikyō War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433497}} * {{ill|Warrior monk|en|Warrior monk|WD=Q7970798|lt=Warrior Monk|1=Warrior monk}}

In Tōdaichū-bō, factions including Ochi, Hashio, and Manzai allied with Southern Court elements such as Sawa and Akiyama emerged, while in Ido, the Tutsui and Toshichi factions prevailed; the rivalry between the Tutsui and Ochi factions eventually split Yamato into two, sparking the conflict known as the "Yamato Eikyō no Ran." In August of the 3rd year of the {{ill|Civilization|en|Civilization|WD=Q8432|lt=Bunmei era|1=Civilization}} (1471), forces led by Fuse (Eastern faction) and Ochi Ie Saka (Western faction) clashed—with Fuse supported by Hashio, Narahara, Kubishira, Toshichi, Tutsui, and Maetaka (Fuse Takada), and Ochi reinforced by Manzai, Hatta, Iitaka, Furuichi, Todata, Koizumi, and Tōtakada (Tōma Takada). With influential guardian daimyō also intervening, the long internal conflict, interspersed with the Ōnin War, eventually ushered in a period of warlord rule during the Sengoku period in Yamato Province.
During the Sengoku period, following the death in battle of {{ill|Hatakeyama clan|en|Hatakeyama clan|WD=Q662881|lt=Hatakeyama clan|1=Hatakeyama clan}} retainer {{ill|Kizawa Nagamasa|en|Kizawa Nagamasa|WD=Q6419175|lt=Kizawa Nagamasa|1=Kizawa Nagamasa}} in Kawachi Province, the clans led by {{ill|Tsutsui clan|en|Tsutsui clan|WD=Q776477|lt=Tutsui Junshō|1=Tsutsui clan}} and {{ill|Ochi Clan|ja|越智氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ochi Clan|1=Ochi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11636555}} expanded their influence. For a time they controlled much of the country and advanced toward becoming warlords, but after Junshō’s sudden death and the succession by his young heir, {{ill|Tutsui Junnin|ja|十市遠康|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tutsui Junnin|1=Tōchi Tōyasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11405031}}, the Ochi and Furuichi clans counterattacked, leading to the disintegration of Tutsui control. Seizing the opportunity, Matsunaga Hisahide, under the Southern Court during the Nanboku-chō period, invaded Yamato and expelled Junnin, subduing the Ochi clan and others, and in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永和|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eiroku era|1=Eiwa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11131293}} (1559) seized the position of provincial protector (Shugo) from Kōfuku-ji. Later, under the Toyotomi regime, Tutsui Junnin reemerged as a warlord in Yamato Province and established a dual system whereby he simultaneously held the status of protector of Yamato Province and acted as the representative of the government-appointed (kanfu) shuto—the temple retainer class—in administering and safeguarding the temple. However, after Junnin's death, Toyotomi Hideyoshi granted Yamato Province to his brother Hidechō, thereby disbanding the existing system, forcing the shuto and the local "kokumin" (the people of the country) to be dissolved; and for Kōfuku-ji, only the original twenty names (as recorded on the official kanfu shuto list) were allowed to serve as temple administrators and security personnel, with a total stipend of 380 koku. == See Also == * {{ill|Yamato Eikyō no Ran|ja|大和永享の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Eikyō no Ran|1=Yamato Eikyō War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433497}} * {{ill|Warrior monk|en|Warrior monk|WD=Q7970798|lt=Warrior Monk|1=Warrior monk}}
